<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define a tensor. How does TensorFlow derive its name from this concept?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **tensor** is a multi-dimensional array generalization of scalars (rank 0), vectors (rank 1), and matrices (rank 2) to **N dimensions**, characterized by attributes *rank*, *shape*, and *dtype*. The name **TensorFlow** is derived from the two core operations the framework performs: **(i) tensors** flow as immutable data structures along graph edges, and **(ii) mathematical operations** (nodes) transform them through a dataflow graph architecture. Thus, "Tensor **Flow**" literally means *"tensors flowing through computational graphs."* (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for rank/shape, 1 for name etymology)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between TensorFlow 1.x and TensorFlow 2.x in terms of execution mode and Keras integration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | TensorFlow 1.x | TensorFlow 2.x |
|---|---|---|
| **Execution Mode** | Static graph; required `tf.Session()` and `tf.Placeholder` to execute | **Eager execution by default**; operations execute immediately |
| **Keras Integration** | External (`pip install keras`, separate library) | **Tightly integrated as `tf.keras`**; official high-level API |
| **Code Verbosity** | High (manual session management) | Low (declarative, Pythonic) |
| **Deployment** | `tf.Graph` and `tf.Session` exports | `SavedModel` format with `model.save()` |

**(3 marks: 1 for execution mode, 1 for Keras integration, 1 for any one additional difference)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of TensorFlow with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of **tensors**, **computational graphs**, and **eager execution** in the framework. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example program, explain how **Keras Sequential API** is used to build, compile, and train a simple feedforward neural network. Mention any one loss function and one optimizer used. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution (a) — TensorFlow Architecture (7 marks)**

**TensorFlow architecture comprises three abstraction layers:**

**1. Frontend (Python API):** High-level interfaces like `tf.keras`, `tf.data`, and low-level APIs for custom graph construction.

**2. Computational Graph Layer:** Represents computations as a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** where:
- **Nodes** = mathematical operations (`tf.add`, `tf.matmul`).
- **Edges** = tensors (multi-dimensional data) flowing between nodes.

**3. Execution Layer (Hardware Backend):** The graph is dispatched to CPU, GPU, or TPU via the **XLA (Accelerated Linear Algebra) compiler** and **CUDA/cuDNN** libraries.

**Role of Tensors:** Tensors are the **data carriers** in TensorFlow. A scalar like `5` is a rank-0 tensor; a 28×28 grayscale image is a rank-2 tensor `(28, 28)`; a batch of 32 such images is a rank-3 tensor `(32, 28, 28)`. They are characterized by:
- **Rank** (number of dimensions)
- **Shape** (size along each dimension)
- **Dtype** (data type, e.g., `float32`)

**Role of Computational Graphs:** The graph **deferred** execution allowed in TF 1.x — the entire computation was defined first, optimized (constant folding, dead-code elimination), and then executed inside a `tf.Session()`. This enabled distributed execution and hardware acceleration but at the cost of debugging difficulty.

**Role of Eager Execution (TF 2.x):** Introduced as default in TF 2.0, eager execution evaluates operations **immediately**, line-by-line, just like ordinary Python code. Benefits:
- **Intuitive debugging** using standard Python tools (`pdb`, `print`).
- **Natural control flow** with `if`, `while`, `for` statements.
- **No need for `tf.Session` or `tf.Placeholder`**.

For users who need graph-mode speed, `tf.function` decorator compiles a Python function into a callable TensorFlow graph, combining the best of both worlds.

**(7 marks: 2 for layers, 2 for tensors, 2 for graphs/eager, 1 for neat diagram)**

### **Model Solution (b) — Keras Sequential API Example (7 marks)**

```python
# Step 1: Import required libraries
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Dense, Flatten
from tensorflow.keras.datasets import mnist

# Step 2: Load and preprocess the MNIST dataset
(x_train, y_train), (x_test, y_test) = mnist.load_data()
x_train, x_test = x_train / 255.0, x_test / 255.0   # Normalize pixel values to [0, 1]

# Step 3: Define the Sequential model (linear stack of layers)
model = Sequential([
    Flatten(input_shape=(28, 28)),       # Input layer: 28x28 -> 784
    Dense(128, activation='relu'),        # Hidden layer 1: 128 neurons, ReLU
    Dense(64, activation='relu'),         # Hidden layer 2: 64 neurons, ReLU
    Dense(10, activation='softmax')       # Output layer: 10 classes, Softmax
])

# Step 4: Compile the model — specify loss, optimizer, and metrics
model.compile(
    optimizer='adam',                                    # Adam optimizer
    loss='sparse_categorical_crossentropy',              # Loss for integer labels
    metrics=['accuracy']
)

# Step 5: Train the model
model.fit(x_train, y_train, epochs=5, batch_size=32, validation_split=0.1)

# Step 6: Evaluate on test data
test_loss, test_acc = model.evaluate(x_test, y_test, verbose=2)
print(f"Test Accuracy: {test_acc:.4f}")
```

**Explanation of Key Steps:**

| Step | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `Sequential([...])` | Creates a linear stack of layers; data flows through layers in order |
| `Flatten(input_shape=(28,28))` | Converts 2D image (28×28) into 1D vector of 784 elements |
| `Dense(128, activation='relu')` | Fully connected layer with 128 neurons; ReLU activation: $f(x)=\max(0,x)$ |
| `Dense(10, activation='softmax')` | Output layer for 10-class classification; converts logits to probabilities |
| `model.compile(...)` | Configures training: **Adam optimizer** (adaptive learning rate) and **sparse categorical cross-entropy loss** (multi-class with integer labels) |
| `model.fit(...)` | Runs training loop for 5 epochs over training data with mini-batches of 32 samples |
| `model.evaluate(...)` | Reports loss and accuracy on unseen test data |

**Loss Function Used:** `sparse_categorical_crossentropy` — combines softmax + cross-entropy; suitable when target labels are integers (`0` to `9`).

**Optimizer Used:** `Adam` — combines momentum (first moment) and RMSProp (second moment) for adaptive, per-parameter learning rates.

**(7 marks: 2 for model definition, 2 for compile + fit, 1 for evaluate, 2 for explanation)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Sequential API** and the **Functional API** of Keras. When is each preferred? Illustrate with one example of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain any **three** common loss functions used in deep learning with their mathematical formulations and the activation functions they are typically paired with. Show why **cross-entropy** is preferred over **MSE** for classification tasks. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution (a) — Sequential vs Functional API (7 marks)**

**Sequential API:**

- **Structure:** Linear stack of layers; each layer has **one input** and **one output** tensor.
- **Syntax:** `model = Sequential([layer1, layer2, ...])`
- **Best for:** Simple feedforward networks, simple CNNs, simple RNNs.
- **Limitations:** Cannot model multi-input, multi-output, shared layers, or skip connections.

**Functional API:**

- **Structure:** Layers are called as functions on tensors; supports arbitrary DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) topologies.
- **Syntax:** Uses `Input()`, layer calls return tensors, `Model(inputs, outputs)` constructs the model.
- **Best for:** ResNet (skip connections), Inception (multi-branch), multi-input/multi-output models, encoder-decoder architectures (e.g., U-Net for medical imaging).
- **Flexibility:** Allows layer weight sharing, residual connections, and non-linear topologies.

**Example — Sequential API:**

```python
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Dense

model = Sequential([
    Dense(64, activation='relu', input_shape=(100,)),
    Dense(32, activation='relu'),
    Dense(10, activation='softmax')
])
```

**Example — Functional API (with skip connection):**

```python
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Input, Dense, Add
from tensorflow.keras.models import Model

inputs = Input(shape=(100,))
x = Dense(64, activation='relu')(inputs)
skip = Dense(64, activation='relu')(inputs)        # Parallel branch
x = Dense(32, activation='relu')(x)
x = Add()([x, skip])                               # Skip connection merges both
outputs = Dense(10, activation='softmax')(x)

model = Model(inputs=inputs, outputs=outputs)
```

**(7 marks: 2 for Sequential, 2 for Functional, 1 for skip connection concept, 2 for examples)**

### **Model Solution (b) — Loss Functions Comparison (7 marks)**

**1. Mean Squared Error (MSE):**

$$L_{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

- **Paired with:** Linear (identity) activation in output layer.
- **Use case:** Regression (predicting continuous values like robot joint angles, motor speed).
- **Gradient:** $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = \dfrac{2}{N}(\hat{y} - y)$.

**2. Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE):**

$$L_{BCE} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\right]$$

- **Paired with:** Sigmoid activation in output layer.
- **Use case:** Binary classification (object vs. background, defective vs. non-defective part).

**3. Categorical Cross-Entropy (CCE):**

$$L_{CCE} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$$

- **Paired with:** Softmax activation in output layer.
- **Use case:** Multi-class classification (digit recognition, gesture classification).

**Why Cross-Entropy is Preferred over MSE for Classification:**

When MSE is paired with sigmoid/softmax activation, the gradient involves the derivative of the activation function:

$$\frac{\partial L_{MSE}}{\partial z} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \sigma'(z)$$

For sigmoid, $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ which becomes **very small** when $\sigma(z)$ is near 0 or 1 (saturation). This causes the **vanishing gradient problem**, slowing or stalling training.

In contrast, with cross-entropy, the gradient simplifies to:

$$\frac{\partial L_{CE}}{\partial z} = \hat{y} - y$$

which is **linear in the error magnitude** — no vanishing gradient. Training is faster, more stable, and converges to better minima. This makes cross-entropy the **default choice** for classification in Keras and TensorFlow.

**(7 marks: 2 for formulas, 2 for activation pairings, 3 for cross-entropy vs MSE justification)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the Gradient Sign** — Writing $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \eta \nabla L$ (this is **ascent**, not descent). **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Wrong Loss–Activation Pairing** — Pairing `categorical_crossentropy` with `sigmoid` (instead of `softmax`) or `binary_crossentropy` with `softmax` (instead of `sigmoid`). **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
> 3. **Forgetting `model.compile()` Before `model.fit()`** — Keras will throw a runtime error. Students often paste code without understanding the lifecycle. **Penalty: 1 mark in "explanation" sub-questions.**
> 4. **Mixing TF 1.x and TF 2.x syntax** — Writing `tf.Session()` in a TF 2.x context. Examiners flag this as **conceptual confusion**. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
> 5. **Not Normalizing Input Data** — Feeding pixel values 0–255 into a neural network without scaling. Causes exploding gradients. Always show `x_train / 255.0`. **Penalty: 1 mark in code-trace questions.**
> 6. **Skipping the Block Diagram** — Part (a) of Question A explicitly demands a *neat block diagram*. A textual description alone loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (TensorFlow + Keras):**

- ✅ **TensorFlow** is a Google-developed open-source library for numerical computation using dataflow graphs; the name comes from *"tensors flowing through graphs."*
- ✅ **Keras** is the official high-level API of TensorFlow (`tf.keras` since v2.0), designed for fast prototyping and minimal boilerplate.
- ✅ A **tensor** has three key attributes: **rank, shape, dtype**.
- ✅ TF 2.x has **eager execution by default**; use `@tf.function` for graph optimization.
- ✅ **Sequential API** = linear stack (one input, one output). **Functional API** = arbitrary DAG, supports multi-input/output, skip connections, weight sharing.
- ✅ Standard pipeline: **Define → Compile → Fit → Evaluate → Predict → Save**.
- ✅ **Loss–activation pairings** are strict: MSE↔Linear, BCE↔Sigmoid, CCE↔Softmax.
- ✅ **Adam** is the default optimizer for most robotic-vision tasks (lr=0.001 default).
- ✅ The **gradient descent update rule** is $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} L(\theta_t)$ — minus sign is **critical**.
- ✅ **Cross-entropy** is preferred over MSE for classification because MSE + sigmoid/softmax produces **vanishing gradients**, whereas CE yields a clean linear gradient $(\hat{y} - y)$.
- ✅ For robotics (PBRUT604), TF/Keras are the **prototype layer** — models are later exported to **TensorRT**, **ONNX**, or **TFLite** for on-robot deployment.
- ✅ Always **normalize inputs** (e.g., `x / 255.0` for images) before training.
- ✅ **TensorBoard** is the visualization tool for tracking loss, accuracy, and computational graphs.
- ✅ **Hardware targets:** CPU (debug), GPU (training), TPU (Google Cloud scale), Edge TPU / Jetson (on-robot inference).
- ✅ Mnemonic: **"T-F-K"** → **Tensors** flow through **graphs**; **Keras** is the friendly steering wheel on top of the TensorFlow engine.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Machine Vision: Basic concepts of image formation, Radiometry and geometric transformations, Geometric Camera Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT604 - SOFT COMPUTING AND COMPUTER VISION FOR ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Industrial Machine Vision: Basic concepts of image formation, Radiometry and geometric transformations, Geometric Camera Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Image Formation in Machine Vision

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Image formation* is the physical and mathematical process by which a 3D scene is projected onto a 2D sensor (CCD/CMOS) through an optical system, producing a measurable irradiance pattern that is then discretized into pixels. It is governed by **geometric optics** (where light goes) and **radiometry** (how much light arrives).

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Dark Room Camera Obscura":**
Imagine you are inside a perfectly sealed, dark shoebox. On one wall, you punch a tiny pinhole. On the opposite wall, an upside-down, dim, full-color picture of the outside world magically appears. This is the **pinhole camera** in its purest form. Every point in the 3D world emits (or reflects) light in straight-line rays; only the ray passing through the pinhole reaches the back wall, painting a faithful but inverted copy of reality. A real industrial camera replaces the pinhole with a **lens** (to gather more light and stay in focus) and the back wall with a digital sensor (a grid of millions of tiny light buckets called *photosites*).

---

### 1.2 Radiometry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Radiometry* is the science of measuring electromagnetic radiation (specifically, visible and near-infrared light) in terms of absolute power, as opposed to *photometry* which weights the same power by the human eye's response. The four foundational radiometric quantities are **Radiant Energy** ($Q$, Joules), **Radiant Flux / Power** ($\Phi$, Watts), **Irradiance** ($E$, W/m²), and **Radiance** ($L$, W/m²·sr).

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Sunlight Bucket Test":**
Hold a 1 m² square cardboard horizontally under the sun. The solar power hitting that square is **irradiance**. Now tilt the cardboard. Less power falls on it because the same "cone of sunlight" is spread over a larger area — this is *Lambert's cosine law*. Now consider how much light travels *in a specific direction* toward your eye from a patch of the cardboard. That directional intensity is **radiance**. Radiance is the "currency" of vision because it is what the camera's aperture actually collects.

---

### 1.3 Geometric Transformations

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Geometric transformations* are mathematical mappings (bijections) that relocate, reorient, or rescale points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ or $\mathbb{R}^3$ while preserving (or systematically altering) geometric structure. In the context of industrial vision, they describe how a 3D world point $P_W$ is rotated and translated into the camera's own coordinate frame $P_C$ via **extrinsic** transformations, and how 3D camera coordinates are mapped to 2D pixel coordinates via **intrinsic** transformations.

**Intuitive Analogy — "GPS Coordinates for the Camera":**
Picture a 3D model of a robot assembly cell in Blender, with origin at the cell's center door. Now place a USB camera on a stand. To tell the camera *exactly* where the calibration target is, you cannot just say "it is 50 cm to the right" — you must first **rotate and slide** the world's coordinate system until it sits exactly where the camera's sensor sits. That "slide-and-spin" is a geometric transformation. Once the coordinate systems match, you can do a final "2D pinhole squish" to project 3D points onto the flat image.

---

### 1.4 Geometric Camera Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A *Geometric Camera Model* is a closed-form mathematical abstraction describing how a point in 3D space is imaged onto a 2D sensor, parameterized by a small set of physically meaningful constants. The two principal KTU-mandated models are:
> 1. **Pinhole Model** — linear, no lens distortion; basis of the *perspective projection matrix* $P = K [R \mid t]$.
> 2. **Thin-Lens Model** — adds focus, depth of field, and aperture; basis of the *lens equation* $\tfrac{1}{f} = \tfrac{1}{v} + \tfrac{1}{u}$.
> More advanced extensions include the **Distorted Pinhole Model** (Brown–Conrady) which adds radial ($k_1, k_2, k_3$) and tangential ($p_1, p_2$) lens distortion coefficients.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Eye vs. The Pinhole":**
Your eye is a sophisticated thin-lens model: the cornea-lens system dynamically changes focal length (a process called *accommodation*) to focus near and far objects on the retina. A pinhole camera has *no lens*, so it has infinite depth of field (everything is in focus) but gathers very little light, making it useless in low light. A *geometric camera model* is the engineer's prescription for "Given a 3D point and a camera, exactly which pixel will light up?" — the cornerstone of every industrial pick-and-place robot, autonomous vehicle, and AR headset.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Image Formation Pipeline (Geometric)

The complete imaging chain proceeds in **four discrete stages**. Understanding the order is critical for KTU problems.

1. **World → Camera (Rigid Body Transformation, 6 DOF)**
   The 3D point is rotated and translated from the world frame to the camera frame. This consumes the *extrinsic* parameters $R$ ($3\times3$ rotation) and $t$ ($3\times1$ translation).

2. **Camera → Normalized Image Plane (Perspective Projection, 2 DOF → 1 DOF)**
   The 3D point is divided by its depth $Z_c$, yielding a 2D point on the *virtual* plane at unit distance in front of the pinhole. This is a non-linear (rational) operation.

3. **Normalized → Pixel Coordinates (Affine Transformation, 5 DOF)**
   The 2D point is scaled (focal length $f_x, f_y$), skewed ($\gamma$), and offset (principal point $c_x, c_y$). This consumes the *intrinsic* parameters in the $K$ matrix.

4. **Distortion Correction (Polynomial, 5 DOF)**
   Real lenses bend straight lines into curves. The *distortion coefficients* $k_1, k_2, k_3, p_1, p_2$ are typically applied *after* projection and *before* pixel mapping during calibration.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these 12 equations — they appear in ~80% of Module 3 derivations.**

| # | Concept | Equation | Symbol Meanings | Units |
|---|---------|----------|-----------------|-------|
| 1 | Thin-Lens Equation | $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{v} + \dfrac{1}{u}$ | $f$ focal length, $v$ image distance, $u$ object distance | metres (m) |
| 2 | Numerical Aperture | $NA = n \sin \theta$ | $n$ refractive index, $\theta$ half-angle of acceptance cone | dimensionless |
| 3 | f-number | $N = \dfrac{f}{D}$ | $f$ focal length, $D$ aperture diameter | dimensionless |
| 4 | Pinhole Projection (x) | $x = f \dfrac{X}{Z}$ | $X, Z$ world coordinates, $f$ focal length | pixels / m |
| 5 | Pinhole Projection (y) | $y = f \dfrac{Y}{Z}$ | $Y, Z$ world coordinates, $f$ focal length | pixels / m |
| 6 | Homogeneous Pinhole | $s \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} R \mid t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_W \\ Y_W \\ Z_W \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $K$ intrinsics, $R$ rotation, $t$ translation, $s$ scale | pixel / world |
| 7 | Intrinsic Matrix $K$ | $K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $f_x, f_y$ focal in pixels, $\gamma$ skew, $c_x, c_y$ principal pt | pixels |
| 8 | Radiant Flux | $\Phi = \dfrac{dQ}{dt}$ | $Q$ energy, $t$ time | W |
| 9 | Irradiance | $E = \dfrac{d\Phi}{dA}$ | $\Phi$ flux on area $A$ | W/m² |
| 10 | Radiance | $L = \dfrac{d^2 \Phi}{dA \, d\Omega \cos \theta}$ | power per area, solid angle, projected angle | W/m²·sr |
| 11 | Lambert Cosine Law | $E = E_0 \cos \theta$ | $E_0$ irradiance at normal, $\theta$ angle from normal | W/m² |
| 12 | Solid Angle of Cone | $\Omega = 2\pi (1 - \cos \theta)$ | $\theta$ half-angle | steradians (sr) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Distinction Trap:** $E$ is for *receiving* surfaces; $M$ (radiant exitance) is for *emitting* surfaces. $L$ (radiance) is invariant along a ray in vacuum — this is *why* we use it for camera equations. $I$ (radiant intensity) is power per *solid angle only* and is used for point sources like LEDs.

---

### 2.3 Radiometric Concepts in Depth

**The Five Pillars of Radiometric Quantities**

- **Radiant Energy $Q$** — The total "stuff" of light, measured in **Joules (J)**. Like the total volume of water in a swimming pool.
- **Radiant Flux $\Phi$** — Energy per unit time, measured in **Watts (W)**. Like the flow rate of water from a tap.
- **Radiant Intensity $I$** — Flux *per solid angle* from a point source, measured in **W/sr**. Like the "directional strength" of a flashlight beam.
- **Irradiance $E$** — Flux *per unit area* incident on a surface, measured in **W/m²**. Like sunlight hitting the ground.
- **Radiance $L$** — The "master quantity": flux per unit area *and* per unit solid angle, in a specific direction. Measured in **W/m²·sr**. It is what an imaging sensor actually captures per pixel.

**Lambertian Surfaces and BRDF**

A *Lambertian surface* reflects light equally in all directions (think: matte white paper, fresh snow, an unpolished ping-pong ball). The *Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function* (BRDF) of a Lambertian surface is a constant:

$$f_r = \dfrac{\rho_d}{\pi}$$

where $\rho_d$ is the *diffuse albedo* (reflectivity, $0 \le \rho_d \le 1$). The $\pi$ in the denominator is what makes shading equations work in computer graphics.

For non-Lambertian (specular or glossy) surfaces, BRDF depends on incoming/outgoing angles and is far more complex (e.g., Cook–Torrance microfacet model).

---

### 2.4 Geometric Transformations in Depth

**2D Transformations (2D Affine Group)**

The general 2D affine transformation in homogeneous coordinates is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a & b & t_x \\ c & d & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

It encompasses:
- **Translation** — $t_x, t_y$ shift the origin.
- **Rotation** — $a = \cos\theta,\ b = -\sin\theta,\ c = \sin\theta,\ d = \cos\theta$.
- **Scaling** — $a = s_x,\ d = s_y$ change size.
- **Shear** — $b$ or $c \ne 0$ skew the figure.
- **Reflection** — Negative scale factors.

**3D Transformations (Special Euclidean Group $SE(3)$)**

A 3D rigid transformation is parameterized by a $3\times3$ rotation matrix $R$ (orthogonal, $\det R = +1$) and a $3\times1$ translation $t$:

$$T = \begin{bmatrix} R & t \\ 0^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{4\times4}$$

Three principal rotation parameterizations (KTU must-know):
1. **Euler Angles** $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ — three sequential rotations; *gimbal lock* singularity when $\theta = \pm 90°$.
2. **Axis-Angle** $(\hat{u}, \alpha)$ — rotation by $\alpha$ around unit axis $\hat{u}$; basis of **Rodrigues' formula**.
3. **Quaternions** $\mathbf{q} = (q_w, q_x, q_y, q_z)$ — singularity-free, double-cover of $SO(3)$.

---

### 2.5 Why This Matters in Industrial Robotics

- **Extrinsic calibration** of a camera mounted on a robot arm enables *visual servoing* — the robot uses the image to physically move toward a target with sub-millimetre precision.
- **Radiometric calibration** enables *defect detection* on shiny metal surfaces, where slight illumination changes can cause false positives.
- **Distortion modelling** is mandatory for *metrology* (measuring object dimensions from images), where uncorrected barrel distortion can produce 5–10% error near image edges.
- **Pinhole assumption** underlies every convolutional neural network in *deep learning-based object detection* (YOLO, Faster R-CNN), making the geometric foundation non-negotiable.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Pinhole Projection Equations (with full justification)

We begin with the **pinhole camera geometry** — a point $P$ in 3D space with coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ in the camera frame, where the $Z$-axis points *out of* the lens, the $X$-axis points *right*, and the $Y$-axis points *down* (standard computer vision convention).

**Step 1 — Setup of similar triangles**

The pinhole (aperture) is at the origin of the camera coordinate frame. Light from $P$ travels in a straight line, passes through the pinhole, and strikes the image plane at $p = (x, y, -f)$, where $f$ is the distance from the pinhole to the image plane (the *focal length*).

The line from $P$ to the pinhole is collinear with the line from $p$ to the pinhole. By the principle of *similar triangles*, the ratio of corresponding sides is preserved:

$$\dfrac{x}{f} = \dfrac{X}{Z}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $x$**

Multiply both sides by $f$:

$$x = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}$$

This is the *horizontal pinhole projection equation*.

**Step 3 — Repeat for the vertical axis**

An identical argument applies to the $Y$-axis:

$$\dfrac{y}{f} = \dfrac{Y}{Z} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad y = f \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z}$$

**Step 4 — Note the sign convention and the inversion**

Because the image plane is conventionally placed *in front* of the camera at $z = f$ (mathematically convenient) rather than behind at $z = -f$ (physically realistic), the image is *not* inverted. If we keep the physical plane at $z = -f$, the equations become $x = -fX/Z$ and $y = -fY/Z$ — this is the source of the "upside-down image" students observe in textbooks.

**Step 5 — Convert to homogeneous coordinates**

To allow the non-linear division by $Z$ to be encoded as a single linear matrix multiplication, we introduce the *scale factor* $s = Z$ and write:

$$\begin{bmatrix} sx \\ sy \\ s \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & f & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the *perspective projection matrix* in its most basic form. Note that the right-hand side is a $4\times1$ vector (3D point in homogeneous form), multiplied by a $3\times4$ matrix (the projection), yielding a $3\times1$ vector whose first two components are the pixel coordinates *up to a common scale* $s = Z$.

---

### 3.2 Full Derivation of the Camera Matrix $P = K[R \mid t]$

This is the **most important derivation** in the entire module. KTU questions frequently ask students to "derive the projection matrix".

**Step 1 — Define the World-to-Camera transformation**

Let $\mathbf{X}_W = (X_W, Y_W, Z_W, 1)^T$ be a point in world coordinates. Its transformation to the camera frame $\mathbf{X}_C$ is a rigid body motion (rotation followed by translation):

$$\mathbf{X}_C = \begin{bmatrix} R & t \\ 0^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{X}_W$$

where $R \in \mathbb{R}^{3\times3}$ is the camera's orientation w.r.t. the world, and $t \in \mathbb{R}^3$ is the camera's position in the world.

Expanding (and discarding the trivial fourth row):

$$\begin{bmatrix} X_C \\ Y_C \\ Z_C \end{bmatrix} = R \begin{bmatrix} X_W \\ Y_W \\ Z_W \end{bmatrix} + t$$

**Step 2 — Apply perspective projection in the camera frame**

From Section 3.1, the pinhole projection of a 3D camera-frame point is:

$$x_{img} = f \dfrac{X_C}{Z_C}, \qquad y_{img} = f \dfrac{Y_C}{Z_C}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to pixel units**

In a real sensor, the pixel spacing is not necessarily equal in $x$ and $y$ (the sensor may have rectangular pixels). We therefore introduce *focal lengths in pixel units*:

$$f_x = \dfrac{f}{s_x}, \qquad f_y = \dfrac{f}{s_y}$$

where $s_x, s_y$ are the physical sizes of one pixel (in metres) in the horizontal and vertical directions.

Additionally, the *principal point* $(c_x, c_y)$ — the pixel coordinate at which the optical axis pierces the sensor — is rarely at the image origin. The full affine transform from sensor millimetres to pixels is:

$$u = f_x \cdot x_{img} + c_x$$
$$v = f_y \cdot y_{img} + c_y$$

Sometimes the sensor axes are not perfectly perpendicular; this is captured by a *skew parameter* $\gamma$:

$$u = f_x \cdot x_{img} + \gamma \cdot y_{img} + c_x$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the intrinsic matrix $K$**

Collecting the constants into a $3\times3$ matrix:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the **camera intrinsic matrix**, a property of the lens-sensor system and independent of where the camera is placed.

**Step 5 — Combine into a single homogeneous projection**

Combining the extrinsic (Step 1) and intrinsic (Step 4) mappings into a single linear operation in homogeneous coordinates:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{K}_{\text{intrinsic}} \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} R \mid t \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{extrinsic}} \begin{bmatrix} X_W \\ Y_W \\ Z_W \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = P \mathbf{X}_W$$

The $3\times4$ matrix $P = K[R \mid t]$ is the **camera projection matrix**. It has 11 degrees of freedom (5 intrinsic, 3 rotation, 3 translation), reduced from 12 by the fact that $P$ is only defined up to scale.

**Final form (display equation):**

$$\boxed{ \; s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} & t_x \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} & t_y \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} & t_z \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_W \\ Y_W \\ Z_W \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \; }$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Thin-Lens Equation

The thin-lens equation is fundamental for any question about **focusing** or **depth of field**.

**Step 1 — Geometric setup**

A thin lens with focal length $f$ has an object at distance $u$ on one side, and a sharp image at distance $v$ on the other. The lens is assumed infinitesimally thin, so all refraction happens at a single plane.

**Step 2 — Similar triangle argument**

The central ray passes through the lens centre undeviated. The chief ray from the top of the object, parallel to the axis, is refracted through the focal point on the image side. The two triangles formed — one between the object and the lens centre, the other between the lens centre and the image — are similar with scale factor $v/u$.

**Step 3 — Algebraic solution**

By similarity, the ray height $h$ at the lens satisfies $h = u \tan\alpha = v \tan\beta$, where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the angles. Combining with the focal point geometry $h = f \tan\alpha$:

$$\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{u} + \dfrac{1}{v}$$

This is the **thin-lens (Gaussian) lens equation**.

**Step 4 — Limiting cases (KTU-favourite)**

- **Object at infinity** ($u \to \infty$): $v = f$. The image forms exactly at the focal plane. This is the basis of *telephoto focusing* on infinity.
- **Object at $2f$** ($u = 2f$): $v = 2f$, and the magnification is $m = v/u = 1$. The image is life-size.
- **Object very close** ($u \to f$): $v \to \infty$. The image is infinitely large (and blurred). This is the basis of a *projector* in reverse.
- **Image distance equals focal length** ($v = f$): $u = \infty$. Confirms Step 4 case 1.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Radiance Invariance Along a Ray

This is the **key insight** that justifies using $L$ in camera equations.

**Step 1 — Setup**

Consider a ray of light passing through two surfaces $A_1$ and $A_2$ at distances $d_1$ and $d_2$ from a point source, with $A_1$ closer. The light spreads into a cone with solid angle $\Omega$.

**Step 2 — Geometric relationship**

The areas of the two surfaces cut by the cone are $A_1$ and $A_2$, related by:

$$\dfrac{A_1}{d_1^2} = \dfrac{A_2}{d_2^2} = \Omega$$

(The solid angle is the projected area divided by the square of the distance — this is the *small-angle approximation*; in general, $dA = r^2 d\Omega$ for a sphere.)

**Step 3 — Power conservation**

The total power $\Phi$ passing through both surfaces is the same (vacuum, no absorption):

$$\Phi = L \cdot A_1 \cdot \Omega = L \cdot A_2 \cdot \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $L$**

Since $A_1 \Omega = A_2 \Omega = \Phi$ and $L$ is defined as $\Phi / (A \cdot \Omega)$:

$$L_1 = L_2 = L$$

**Conclusion:** Radiance is *conserved* along a ray in vacuum. This is why a camera can measure radiance from a distant object and the result is independent of the distance — a foundational fact in remote sensing, photogrammetry, and computer vision.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Concept to Industrial Application

| Concept | Mathematical Tool | Typical KTU Industrial Application | Difficulty |
|---------|-------------------|------------------------------------|------------|
| Pinhole Projection | $x = fX/Z$ | Lens-free imaging, high-speed motion analysis | Low |
| Camera Matrix $P$ | $K[R \mid t]$ | Robot hand-eye calibration, AR/VR pose estimation | High |
| Thin-Lens Equation | $1/f = 1/u + 1/v$ | Selecting machine vision lens for working distance | Medium |
| Lambert Cosine Law | $E = E_0 \cos\theta$ | Designing uniform illumination for defect detection | Low |
| Radiance Invariance | $L_1 = L_2$ | Hyperspectral imaging, satellite image interpretation | Medium |
| Radial Distortion | $x_d = x(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$ | Wide-angle automotive cameras, fisheye correction | High |
| Rodrigues' Formula | $R = I + \sin\theta [u]_\times + (1-\cos\theta)[u]_\times^2$ | Axis-angle to rotation matrix conversion | High |

    G --> H[Distortion Correction<br/>k1 k2 p1 p2]
    H --> I[Intrinsic Transform<br/>K Matrix]

    J --> K[Robot Control<br/>Pick and Place]

> **Reading Guide:** This flowchart shows the *causal chain* of how photons from a real-world object become a robot action. Each box represents a *transformation* (geometric, radiometric, electronic, or computational). KTU problems often ask students to identify *which stage* a particular error originates in.

### 4.2 Pinhole Camera Geometry — Coordinate Frame Relationships

    C -->|Perspective Divide<br/>by ZC| N[Normalized Image Plane<br/>x y at z equals 1]
    N -->|Intrinsic K<br/>focal length and offset| P[Pixel Frame<br/>u v in pixels]

### 4.3 Solid Angle and Radiance Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SourceBlock[Radiating Patch]
        SRC[Surface Element dA<br/>Emitting with Radiance L]
    end

    SRC -->|Cone of half angle theta| OBS1[Observer 1<br/>at distance r1]
    SRC -->|Same radiance L| OBS2[Observer 2<br/>at distance r2]
    SRC -->|Same radiance L| OBS3[Observer 3<br/>at distance r3]

    OBS1 -->|Measures L| C1[Camera pixel value]
    OBS2 -->|Measures L| C2[Camera pixel value]
    OBS3 -->|Measures L| C3[Camera pixel value]

    C1 --> EQ[All three pixels<br/>record identical L]
    C2 --> EQ
    C3 --> EQ

    style SRC fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ff8f00

```

> **Reading Guide:** This is a *visual proof* of the Radiance Invariance Theorem (Section 3.4). It explains why a colour meter placed 1 m from a painted wall gives the *same* radiance reading as one placed 10 m away (in vacuum / clean air).

---

### 4.4 Transformation Chain in Extrinsic Calibration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pattern Point PW<br/>Known from CAD] -->|Known 3D| T1[Apply Extrinsic RT]
    T1 --> Q1[Projected to PC<br/>In Camera Frame]
    Q1 -->|Perspective Divide| Q2[2D point on normalized plane]
    Q2 -->|Apply K| Q3[2D point in pixels]
    Q3 -->|Compare with detected<br/>corner in image| ERR[Reprojection Error]
    ERR -->|Nonlinear optimization<br/>Levenberg Marquardt| UPD[Update RT and K]
    UPD --> T1

    style P1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style ERR fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style UPD fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is the *iterative loop* of Zhang's method (the most famous camera calibration algorithm, KTU Module 3 essential). The reprojection error is minimized over a chessboard of $\ge 10$ images to recover both $K$ and $R, t$.
