## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]
*With a neat diagram, explain the principle of image formation in a pinhole camera. State two major limitations of the pinhole camera and explain how a lens overcomes them.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Principle (1.5 marks):** A pinhole camera consists of a light-tight box with a tiny aperture on one face. Light rays from a 3D scene pass through the aperture in straight lines and strike the opposite face (the image plane). By similar triangles, a 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ is imaged at $p = (fX/Z, fY/Z)$ on the image plane located at distance $f$ from the pinhole. The result is an *inverted, scaled* 2D reproduction of the 3D scene.

**Two limitations and lens-based remedies (1.5 marks):**
1. **Poor light gathering:** The pinhole has an infinitesimally small area, so very few photons reach each point on the image plane, requiring long exposure times. A **lens** of diameter $D$ collects light from a much larger area, increasing irradiance by a factor $(D/d_h)^2$ where $d_h$ is the pinhole diameter.
2. **No focusing control:** Every object distance produces a sharp image only at a specific $v = fZ/(Z-f)$. A **thin lens** of variable focal length (or movable position) can be focused to satisfy $1/f = 1/u + 1/v$ for the chosen object distance.

**Diagram Description:** A box with a small hole on the left, an inverted arrow object outside, and an inverted arrow image formed on the right inner wall. Rays from the arrow tip and tail cross at the pinhole.

---

**Question A2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]
*Define the terms (i) Radiance, (ii) Irradiance, and (iii) BRDF. State their SI units.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Quantity | Definition | SI Unit |
|----------|------------|---------|
| (i) **Radiance $L$** | Radiant flux emitted, reflected, transmitted, or received by a surface *per unit projected area per unit solid angle* in a given direction. | W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹ |
| (ii) **Irradiance $E$** | Radiant flux *incident* on a surface per unit area. It is the *surface-density* of received power. | W·m⁻² |
| (iii) **BRDF $f_r$** | Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function — the ratio of outgoing radiance in a given direction to the incoming irradiance from another direction. It characterises how a surface reflects light. | sr⁻¹ |

**Key Distinction (often tested):** Radiance is *directional*; irradiance is *hemi-spherical* (integrated over the incoming hemisphere). For a Lambertian surface, $f_r = \rho_d / \pi$ is a constant in sr⁻¹.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Camera Geometry and Radiometry (14 Marks)**

**Part (a)** [7 Marks] — *Derive the pinhole camera projection equations, starting from the geometry of similar triangles, and show how the intrinsic matrix $K$ is constructed. State the physical meaning of each element of $K$.*

**Part (b)** [7 Marks] — *A machine vision camera has a lens of focal length $f = 25$ mm. It is used to inspect a printed circuit board (PCB) of size $200$ mm $\times 150$ mm, placed at an object distance of $u = 300$ mm from the lens.*
- *(i) Find the image distance $v$ at which the sensor must be placed.*
- *(ii) Calculate the required sensor size (in mm) to capture the entire PCB.*
- *(iii) If the f-number is $N = 2.8$, find the aperture diameter $D$ and the numerical aperture $NA$ (assume $n = 1$ for air).*

---

**Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Full Derivation (7 Marks)**

*Setup (1 mark):* Place the pinhole at the origin of a coordinate system. The image plane is at $z = f$. A 3D point has camera coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$. The image point is $(x, y)$ on the plane.

*Similar triangles (2 marks):* Drawing the line from the 3D point to the pinhole and continuing to the image plane, the $X$-$Z$ triangle is similar to the $x$-$f$ triangle. Hence:

$$\dfrac{x}{f} = \dfrac{X}{Z} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \dfrac{y}{f} = \dfrac{Y}{Z}$$

*Intrinsic Matrix Construction (3 marks):* In pixel coordinates, $f$ must be expressed in *pixels per metre* as $f_x = f/s_x$ and $f_y = f/s_y$. The image origin is shifted to the principal point $(c_x, c_y)$. Including skew $\gamma$:

$$u = f_x \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z} + \gamma \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z} + c_x, \qquad v = f_y \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z} + c_y$$

Written in homogeneous form with $s = Z$:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{K} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

*Physical meaning (1 mark):*
- $f_x, f_y$: focal length in pixel units (horizontal and vertical).
- $c_x, c_y$: pixel coordinates of the principal point (where the optical axis intersects the sensor).
- $\gamma$: skew factor modelling non-rectangular pixels; typically $\approx 0$ for modern sensors.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

*Given:* $f = 25$ mm, $u = 300$ mm, PCB size $= 200 \times 150$ mm, $N = 2.8$, $n = 1$.

*(i) Image distance $v$ (2 marks):*
Use the thin-lens equation:

$$\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{25} - \dfrac{1}{300}$$

Compute common denominator:

$$\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{12}{300} - \dfrac{1}{300} = \dfrac{11}{300}$$

Therefore:

$$v = \dfrac{300}{11} \approx 27.27 \text{ mm}$$

*(ii) Sensor size (2.5 marks):*
The lateral magnification is:

$$m = \dfrac{v}{u} = \dfrac{27.27}{300} \approx 0.0909$$

The image of the PCB on the sensor is:

$$\text{Width} = 200 \times 0.0909 \approx 18.18 \text{ mm}$$
$$\text{Height} = 150 \times 0.0909 \approx 13.64 \text{ mm}$$

So the sensor must be at least **18.2 mm $\times$ 13.7 mm** (a 1-inch sensor of nominal 16 mm diagonal is too small; a 2/3-inch sensor at 11 mm diagonal is far too small — a 4/3-inch or APS-C sensor is needed).

*(iii) Aperture and NA (2.5 marks):*

$$D = \dfrac{f}{N} = \dfrac{25}{2.8} \approx 8.93 \text{ mm}$$

The numerical aperture (for small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = D/(2f)$):

$$NA = n \sin\theta = 1 \times \dfrac{D}{2f} = \dfrac{8.93}{50} \approx 0.179$$

---

#### **Question B — Geometric Transformations and Distortion (14 Marks)** *[Internal Choice]*

**Part (a)** [7 Marks] — *Explain the concept of extrinsic camera parameters. Derive the complete camera projection matrix $P = K[R \mid t]$ relating a 3D world point to a 2D pixel coordinate. State clearly the role of the extrinsic parameters $(R, t)$ in robotic vision.*

**Part (b)** [7 Marks] — *In a robotic pick-and-place cell, a chessboard calibration target is placed such that its corner points have world coordinates as given in the table below. The camera intrinsics have been pre-calibrated as $f_x = 800$, $f_y = 800$, $c_x = 320$, $c_y = 240$, with zero skew. The estimated extrinsic parameters are $R = I$ (identity) and $t = [0, 0, 500]^T$ (in mm).*

| Point | $X_W$ (mm) | $Y_W$ (mm) | $Z_W$ (mm) |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| A | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| B | 30 | 0 | 0 |
| C | 0 | 30 | 0 |
| D | 30 | 30 | 0 |

- *(i) Compute the 2D pixel coordinates of points A, B, C, and D using the projection equations.*
- *(ii) If the camera is rotated by 30° about the world $Y$-axis (i.e., $R_y(30°)$), recompute the projection of point B. Assume translation remains $t = [0, 0, 500]^T$.*

---

**Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Extrinsic Parameters and Full Matrix (7 Marks)**

*Concept (2 marks):* Extrinsic parameters describe the position and orientation of the camera *with respect to the world coordinate frame*. They convert world coordinates to camera coordinates through a rigid body transformation (rotation + translation). The rotation matrix $R$ (orthogonal, $\det R = 1$) encodes orientation (3 DOF), and the translation vector $t$ (3 DOF) encodes position — 6 DOF total.

*Derivation (4 marks):* A world point $\mathbf{X}_W = (X_W, Y_W, Z_W, 1)^T$ in homogeneous form is first mapped to camera coordinates:

$$\mathbf{X}_C = \begin{bmatrix} R & t \\ 0^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{X}_W$$

The camera coordinates then undergo perspective projection (dividing by $Z_C$) and the intrinsic transformation (multiplying by $K$):

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} R & t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_W \\ Y_W \\ Z_W \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = P \mathbf{X}_W$$

The full camera matrix is $P = K [R \mid t]$, a $3 \times 4$ matrix. It has 11 DOF: 5 intrinsic (in $K$) + 3 rotation + 3 translation. The DOF reduces from 12 because $P$ is defined only up to a non-zero scalar multiple.

*Role in robotic vision (1 mark):* Extrinsic parameters are essential for *hand-eye calibration* and *visual servoing* — they allow the robot to convert 2D image measurements into 3D world coordinates, which it then uses to compute the joint angles needed to reach a target.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

*Given:* $K = \begin{bmatrix} 800 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 800 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $R = I_3$, $t = [0, 0, 500]^T$ mm.

*(i) Project all four points (4 marks):*

Since $R = I$ and $t = [0, 0, 500]^T$, the camera coordinates are simply:

$$X_C = X_W, \quad Y_C = Y_W, \quad Z_C = Z_W + 500$$

For the four points ($Z_W = 0$ for all, so $Z_C = 500$):

**Point A** $(0, 0, 0)$:

$$u = 800 \cdot \dfrac{0}{500} + 320 = 320, \quad v = 800 \cdot \dfrac{0}{500} + 240 = 240$$

**Point B** $(30, 0, 0)$:

$$u = 800 \cdot \dfrac{30}{500} + 320 = 48 + 320 = 368, \quad v = 800 \cdot \dfrac{0}{500} + 240 = 240$$

**Point C** $(0, 30, 0)$:

$$u = 800 \cdot \dfrac{0}{500} + 320 = 320, \quad v = 800 \cdot \dfrac{30}{500} + 240 = 48 + 240 = 288$$

**Point D** $(30, 30, 0)$:

$$u = 800 \cdot \dfrac{30}{500} + 320 = 368, \quad v = 800 \cdot \dfrac{30}{500} + 240 = 288$$

| Point | $(u, v)$ pixels |
|-------|----------------|
| A | (320, 240) |
| B | (368, 240) |
| C | (320, 288) |
| D | (368, 288) |

*(ii) Project point B after rotation by 30° about $Y$-axis (3 marks):*

The rotation matrix about $Y$-axis by angle $\theta = 30°$ is:

$$R_y(30°) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 30° & 0 & \sin 30° \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin 30° & 0 & \cos 30° \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 & 0 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -0.5 & 0 & 0.866 \end{bmatrix}$$

For point B = $(30, 0, 0, 1)^T$:

$$X_C = 0.866(30) + 0(0) + 0.5(0) = 25.98$$
$$Y_C = 0(30) + 1(0) + 0(0) = 0$$
$$Z_C = -0.5(30) + 0(0) + 0.866(0) + 500 = -15 + 500 = 485$$

Project to pixels:

$$u = 800 \cdot \dfrac{25.98}{485} + 320 = 42.85 + 320 = 362.85$$
$$v = 800 \cdot \dfrac{0}{485} + 240 = 240$$

**Point B'** projects to approximately **(363, 240)** pixels.

*Physical interpretation:* The point moved *closer* in $Z$ (from 500 to 485) and *inward* in $X$ (from 30 to 25.98). Its $u$ coordinate moved from 368 to 363 — closer to the principal point, as expected when the chessboard is rotated.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Module 3, 2024 Scheme)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $s = Z$ factor:** Many students drop the homogeneous scale factor $s$ when writing the projection, losing 1–2 marks per sub-part. The final form **must** have the $s$ on the left.
> 2. **Confusing $E$ and $L$:** A frequent error is writing irradiance where radiance is meant. Remember: $E$ is *total incoming*; $L$ is *directional*.
> 3. **Inverting the image:** Students often forget that the pinhole image is *upside down*. The negative signs in $x = -fX/Z$, $y = -fY/Z$ are routinely missed, costing full marks in derivation questions.
> 4. **Units in the thin-lens equation:** $f$, $u$, $v$ must be in the *same* units. Mixing mm and cm is a silent killer. Always convert before substitution.
> 5. **Skewness $\gamma$:** Most students write $K$ as diagonal. KTU rewards the *correct* general form with skew (even if $\gamma = 0$ is plugged in later).
> 6. **Sign of rotation:** Clockwise vs. counter-clockwise rotations about each axis follow the *right-hand rule*. A sign error here propagates to all subsequent extrinsic calculations.
> 7. **Extrinsic DOF:** Writing "extrinsic = 6 DOF" without explaining *why* (3 rotation + 3 translation) is worth only partial marks. Always state the breakdown.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and read it 30 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

#### **Core Concepts (must know verbatim)**
- ☐ Pinhole camera principle: light passes through an aperture, inverting the image.
- ☐ Pinhole projection equations: $x = fX/Z$, $y = fY/Z$.
- ☐ Thin-lens equation: $1/f = 1/v + 1/u$.
- ☐ f-number: $N = f/D$.
- ☐ Numerical aperture: $NA = n \sin\theta$.
- ☐ Radiance definition: $L = d^2\Phi / (dA \cdot d\Omega \cos\theta)$.
- ☐ Irradiance definition: $E = d\Phi / dA$.
- ☐ Lambert cosine law: $E = E_0 \cos\theta$.
- ☐ BRDF for Lambertian surface: $f_r = \rho_d / \pi$.

#### **Camera Matrix (must derive on demand)**
- ☐ World-to-camera: $X_C = R X_W + t$.
- ☐ Camera-to-pixel: $s \tilde{\mathbf{x}} = K \mathbf{X}_C$.
- ☐ Full matrix: $P = K [R \mid t]$ (11 DOF).
- ☐ Intrinsic matrix $K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
- ☐ Physical meaning of each $K$ element.

#### **Transformations (must classify and apply)**
- ☐ 2D affine transformations: translation, rotation, scaling, shear, reflection.
- ☐ 3D rigid transformations: rotation matrices about $X, Y, Z$ axes.
- ☐ Euler angles vs. axis-angle vs. quaternions (be aware of gimbal lock).
- ☐ Homogeneous coordinates: convert 2D point $(x, y)$ to $(x, y, 1)$ and 3D point $(X, Y, Z)$ to $(X, Y, Z, 1)$.

#### **Radiometric Laws (must state and use)**
- ☐ Radiance is *invariant* along a ray in vacuum ($L_1 = L_2$).
- ☐ Lambert's cosine law for tilted surfaces.
- ☐ Inverse square law for point sources (debatable in KTU scope — focus on Lambert).

#### **Distortion (must recognise the equation)**
- ☐ Radial distortion: $x_d = x(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$.
- ☐ Tangential distortion: $x_d = x + 2p_1 xy + p_2(r^2 + 2x^2)$.
- ☐ Barrel vs. pincushion identification from signs of $k_1, k_2$.

#### **Numerical Fluency (must solve in <5 min)**
- ☐ Thin-lens equation for $v$ given $f$ and $u$.
- ☐ Pinhole projection for $u, v$ given $X_W, Y_W, Z_W$ and $K, R, t$.
- ☐ Sensor size given field of view and object size.
- ☐ f-number to aperture diameter conversion.
- ☐ Lambertian irradiance for a tilted surface.

#### **High-Yield One-Liners for KTU Theory**
- ☐ *Pinhole* has infinite depth of field; *thin-lens* has finite depth of field.
- ☐ *Intrinsics* are properties of the lens-sensor; *extrinsics* are properties of the camera's pose.
- ☐ *Radiance* is the only radiometric quantity preserved along a ray in vacuum.
- ☐ *Distortion* is a non-linear effect; *intrinsic projection* is a linear (homogeneous) effect.
- ☐ *Principal point* is the projection of the optical centre on the sensor; it is *not* necessarily at the image centre due to manufacturing tolerances.
- ☐ *Skew* $\gamma$ is zero for most modern sensors but kept in the model for completeness.
- ☐ *Zhang's method* uses a planar chessboard and produces $K$ plus per-image $[R \mid t]$ — the most popular calibration algorithm in industry.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Industrial Machine Vision: Image Formation, Radiometry, Geometric Transformations, and Camera Models.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pinhole camera model, Perspective projection and camera parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT604 - SOFT COMPUTING AND COMPUTER VISION FOR ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pinhole camera model, Perspective projection and camera parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Pinhole Camera Model** is the fundamental geometric model used in computer vision to describe the mathematical mapping between a 3D scene point in the world coordinate system and its corresponding 2D projection on the image (sensor) plane. It is an idealized model in which all light rays from a scene pass through a single infinitesimally small aperture (the **pinhole**) before striking the image plane located at a distance equal to the **focal length** ($f$) from the aperture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The pinhole camera approximates a real camera by assuming no lens, only a single point aperture. It forms the foundational abstraction for **perspective projection** and the derivation of both **intrinsic** and **extrinsic** camera parameters.

The model is characterized by:
- A **Center of Projection (CoP)** also called the optical center or pinhole.
- An **Image Plane** where the inverted real image is formed.
- A **Principal Axis** perpendicular to the image plane passing through CoP.
- A **Principal Point** where the principal axis meets the image plane.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Camera Obscura:**
> Imagine a completely dark room (camera obscura) with a tiny hole in one wall. Outside the room, a tree is illuminated by sunlight. Light rays from the tree travel in straight lines through the tiny hole and project an **inverted, scaled-down image** of the tree on the opposite wall inside the room. This is exactly how a pinhole camera works. The size of the projected image is directly proportional to the focal length $f$. A larger room (larger $f$) yields a larger projected image, but the brightness is reduced because the same light energy is spread over a larger area.

**Intuitive Summary:**
- The 3D world has **three dimensions** ($X, Y, Z$).
- The image captures only **two dimensions** ($u, v$).
- The **depth** $Z$ is lost during projection, but it controls the **scale** of the projected point (closer objects appear larger).
- The mapping is **non-linear** because of the division by $Z$ (perspective division).

### 1.3 Key Physical and Mathematical Constants

| Constant | Symbol | Units | Standard Value / Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Focal length | $f$ | millimeters (mm) or pixels | Typically **35 mm to 50 mm** for standard cameras; **3 mm to 10 mm** for webcams |
| Pixel size | $\Delta u, \Delta v$ | $\mu m/pixel$ | Typically **1.4 to 10 $\mu m$** |
| Image resolution | $W \times H$ | pixels | e.g., **1920 $\times$ 1080** |
| Principal point | $(c_x, c_y)$ | pixels | Usually near image center $(W/2, H/2)$ |
| Field of View | $\theta$ | degrees | Depends on $f$ and sensor size |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** In the KTU board exam, examiners expect you to state that the pinhole model is the **simplest valid model** for perspective imaging and is used to derive the **camera matrix (calibration matrix)** $K$ in homogeneous coordinates.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Breakdown

The pinhole camera model can be decomposed into four sequential transformations:

**Step 1: World-to-Camera Transformation (Rigid Body Motion)**
- A 3D point in the world $\mathbf{P}_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w)^T$ must first be expressed in the camera's own coordinate frame.
- This requires a rotation $\mathbf{R} \in SO(3)$ (a 3×3 orthonormal matrix) and a translation $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^3$.

$$
\mathbf{P}_c = \mathbf{R} \, \mathbf{P}_w + \mathbf{t}
$$

**Step 2: Perspective Projection (Non-linear)**
- The camera-coordinate point $\mathbf{P}_c = (X_c, Y_c, Z_c)^T$ is projected onto the image plane by ray-line geometry.
- Only rays passing through the pinhole contribute; this enforces a **division by $Z_c$** (the depth).

**Step 3: Metric-to-Pixel Conversion (Intrinsic Mapping)**
- The image-plane coordinates $(x, y)$ in metric units (mm) are converted to pixel indices $(u, v)$ using the focal length expressed in pixels $(f_x, f_y)$ and the principal point offset $(c_x, c_y)$.

**Step 4: Homogeneous Coordinate Formulation**
- All three transformations are combined into a single **3×4 camera projection matrix** $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{K} \, [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$, enabling a linear operation in homogeneous coordinates.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master the equations below — they constitute 60–70% of all marks in Module 3 derivations.**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x = f \cdot \dfrac{X_c}{Z_c}$ | Perspective projection on $x$-axis | pixels |
| 2 | $y = f \cdot \dfrac{Y_c}{Z_c}$ | Perspective projection on $y$-axis | pixels |
| 3 | $u = f_x \cdot \dfrac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x$ | Pixel $u$-coordinate | pixels |
| 4 | $v = f_y \cdot \dfrac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$ | Pixel $v$-coordinate | pixels |
| 5 | $s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \, [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Full homogeneous projection | pixels |
| 6 | $\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Intrinsic matrix | pixels |
| 7 | $f_x = f / \Delta u, \quad f_y = f / \Delta v$ | Focal length in pixels | pixels |
| 8 | $X_c = r_{11} X_w + r_{12} Y_w + r_{13} Z_w + t_x$ | Rotation + translation $X$ | mm or m |
| 9 | $Y_c = r_{21} X_w + r_{22} Y_w + r_{23} Z_w + t_y$ | Rotation + translation $Y$ | mm or m |
| 10 | $Z_c = r_{31} X_w + r_{32} Y_w + r_{33} Z_w + t_z$ | Rotation + translation $Z$ | mm or m |
| 11 | $\mathbf{P}_c = \mathbf{R} \, \mathbf{P}_w + \mathbf{t}$ | Extrinsic transformation | mm or m |
| 12 | $s = Z_c$ (the depth) | Scale factor in homogeneous form | mm or m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Strict notation rule:** Use $\vert X_c \vert$ or $\mid Z_c \mid$ — never the bare pipe symbol `|` — inside any markdown table or it will break the rendering. The same applies to absolute values and determinants.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The pinhole camera model is the **mathematical backbone** of:

- **Robotic Vision (your course):** Used for visual servoing, object localization, SLAM, and pick-and-place tasks.
- **Augmented Reality (AR):** Projects virtual 3D objects into the real camera view to align them physically.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Used in stereo and monocular depth estimation.
- **Medical Imaging:** Endoscopes and laparoscopes rely on this model to reconstruct surgical scenes.
- **Industrial Metrology:** 3D scanning and reverse engineering depend on calibration using this exact model.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Similar Triangles to the Basic Pinhole Equation

**Setup:** Consider a 3D point $\mathbf{P}_w = (X, Y, Z)^T$ in the camera coordinate system (we temporarily assume the world and camera frames coincide). The image plane is located at distance $f$ in front of the pinhole. The light ray from $\mathbf{P}_w$ passes through the pinhole and intersects the image plane at $(x, y, f)$.

**Step 1:** Form two similar triangles in the $X$–$Z$ plane.

- Triangle 1: vertices at origin (pinhole), $(X, 0, Z)$, and $(0, 0, 0)$.
- Triangle 2: vertices at origin (pinhole), $(x, 0, f)$, and $(0, 0, 0)$.

**Step 2:** By the property of similar triangles, the ratios of corresponding sides are equal.

$$
\frac{x}{f} = \frac{X}{Z}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $x$.

$$
x = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}
$$

**Step 4:** Repeat the same logic in the $Y$–$Z$ plane.

$$
y = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}
$$

**Step 5:** Combine both equations into the **perspective projection** result.

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \frac{f}{Z} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6:** Interpret: the **scaling factor** $f/Z$ is **inversely proportional to depth**. A point twice as far ($Z \to 2Z$) appears half as large. This is the geometric origin of **foreshortening**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The division by $Z$ is the **key non-linearity** that distinguishes perspective projection from orthographic (weak-perspective) projection. Always highlight this in your exam answer for full marks.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Conversion from Image-Plane Metric Units to Pixel Coordinates

**Setup:** The image plane coordinates $(x, y)$ obtained above are in metric units (e.g., millimeters). The digital image sensor is divided into discrete pixels of sizes $\Delta u$ (horizontal) and $\Delta v$ (vertical), and the principal point is offset from the top-left pixel origin by $(c_x, c_y)$ pixels.

**Step 1:** Convert metric $x$ to pixel $u$ by dividing by the pixel pitch and applying the principal-point offset.

$$
u = \frac{x}{\Delta u} + c_x
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $x = f X_c / Z_c$ and define $f_x = f / \Delta u$ (focal length in pixel units along the $u$-axis).

$$
u = f_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x
$$

**Step 3:** Repeat for the vertical axis with $f_y = f / \Delta v$.

$$
v = f_y \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y
$$

**Step 4:** Introduce the **skew parameter** $s$ (used when the sensor axes are not perfectly perpendicular).

$$
u = f_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + s \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_x
$$

**Step 5:** Write the four equations in **homogeneous form**. Let $s = Z_c$ (the scale).

$$
s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the $3 \times 3$ matrix as the **intrinsic camera matrix** $\mathbf{K}$.

$$
\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Full World-to-Image Homogeneous Projection

**Step 1:** Convert world point $\mathbf{P}_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w)^T$ to homogeneous form.

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{P}}_w = \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the rigid-body transformation using a $3 \times 4$ extrinsic matrix.

$$
\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} & t_x \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} & t_y \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} & t_z \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Concatenate the intrinsic and extrinsic matrices into the **camera projection matrix** $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{K} \, [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$, which is $3 \times 4$.

**Step 4:** Write the final compact form.

$$
s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{M} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the **perspective divide** (divide by the third homogeneous coordinate) to recover the pixel coordinates.

$$
u = \frac{m_{11} X_w + m_{12} Y_w + m_{13} Z_w + m_{14}}{m_{31} X_w + m_{32} Y_w + m_{33} Z_w + m_{34}}
$$

$$
v = \frac{m_{21} X_w + m_{22} Y_w + m_{23} Z_w + m_{24}}{m_{31} X_w + m_{32} Y_w + m_{33} Z_w + m_{34}}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Degrees of Freedom of the Pinhole Model

| Parameter Group | Symbol | DoF | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Focal length $x$ | $f_x$ | 1 | Distance from CoP to image plane in pixel units |
| Focal length $y$ | $f_y$ | 1 | Separate horizontal/vertical focal length |
| Skew | $s$ | 1 | Shear between sensor axes (often 0 in modern CMOS) |
| Principal point | $c_x, c_y$ | 2 | Image coordinates of the optical center |
| Rotation | $\mathbf{R}$ | 3 | Three independent Euler / Rodrigues parameters |
| Translation | $\mathbf{t}$ | 3 | Three Cartesian offsets |
| **Total** | — | **11** | **Minimum DoF for a complete pinhole model** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why 11 DoF?** Intrinsic contributes $1 + 1 + 1 + 2 = 5$ parameters, extrinsic contributes $3 + 3 = 6$ parameters. $5 + 6 = 11$. This number is crucial for the **Zhang's calibration method** (covered later in the module), which requires a minimum of 6 known points to solve using a $3 \times 3$ homography per image.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: End-to-End Pinhole Camera Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[World Point Pw] --> B[Extrinsic Transform]
    B --> C[Camera Coordinates Pc]
    C --> D[Perspective Projection Divide by Zc]
    D --> E[Metric Image Plane xy]
    E --> F[Intrinsic Transform via K]
    F --> G[Pixel Coordinates uv]
    G --> H[Digital Image]

    subgraph EXTRINSIC["Extrinsic Parameters R t"]
        B
    end

    subgraph INTRINSIC["Intrinsic Parameters K = fx s cx 0 fy cy 0 0 1"]
        F
    end

    A2[3D Scene] -. Light rays through pinhole .-> D
```

### 4.2 Schematic Flow: Camera Parameter Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Camera Parameters] --> P1[Intrinsic Parameters]
    P0 --> P2[Extrinsic Parameters]
    P0 --> P3[Distortion Parameters]

    P1 --> P1a[Focal Length fx fy]
    P1 --> P1b[Principal Point cx cy]
    P1 --> P1c[Skew Coefficient s]
    P1 --> P1d[Matrix K 3x3]

    P2 --> P2a[Rotation Matrix R 3x3]
    P2 --> P2b[Translation Vector t 3x1]

    P3 --> P3a[Radial Distortion k1 k2 k3]
    P3 --> P3b[Tangential Distortion p1 p2]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Geometric Ray Diagram of the Pinhole Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SCENE["3D World Space"]
        P1[Point P at X Y Z]
    end

    subgraph APERTURE["Pinhole Aperture at Origin"]
        O[Optical Center CoP 0 0 0]
    end

    subgraph IMAGE["Image Plane at distance f from CoP"]
        IM[Projected Point x y on image plane]
    end

    P1 -. Straight light ray through CoP .-> O
    O -. Continues to image plane .-> IM
    IM --> INV[Image is INVERTED relative to scene]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Solving the Camera Matrix Equation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Known 3D World Points Xw Yw Zw] --> SOLVE[Camera Calibration Algorithm Zhang's Method]
    IN2[Corresponding 2D Image Points u v] --> SOLVE
    SOLVE --> OUT1[Intrinsic Matrix K]
    SOLVE --> OUT2[Extrinsic R and t]
    SOLVE --> OUT3[Distortion Coefficients]

    OUT1 --> USE[Used for 3D Reconstruction]
    OUT2 --> USE
    OUT3 --> USE
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
Define the **pinhole camera model**. List its principal components.

**Model Answer:**
The pinhole camera model is an idealized mathematical model of a camera that describes the imaging geometry using a single point aperture (the **pinhole** or **center of projection**). Light rays from a 3D scene pass through this point and form an **inverted** image on the image plane located at a distance equal to the **focal length $f$**.

Principal components are:
1. **Center of Projection (CoP)** – the pinhole / optical center.
2. **Image Plane** – where the image is formed, at distance $f$ from CoP.
3. **Principal Axis** – line through CoP perpendicular to the image plane.
4. **Principal Point** – intersection of the principal axis with the image plane.
5. **Focal Length $f$** – distance between CoP and the image plane.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
What are **intrinsic** and **extrinsic** camera parameters? Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer:**
- **Intrinsic parameters** describe the internal optical and geometric properties of the camera, independent of its position in the world. Examples: focal length $(f_x, f_y)$ and principal point $(c_x, c_y)$.
- **Extrinsic parameters** describe the position and orientation of the camera in the world coordinate system. Examples: rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ and translation vector $\mathbf{t}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not skip the similar-triangles derivation** — most students write $x = fX/Z$ without justification, losing **3 of 14 marks**.
> 2. **Always state the units explicitly** (mm, pixels, m) — failing to do so costs 1 mark per sub-part.
> 3. **Confusion between $f$ (mm) and $f_x$ (pixels)** is a common error; clarify the conversion $f_x = f / \Delta u$.
> 4. In the extrinsic matrix, write out the **full 3×4 form** at least once — a 3×3 $\mathbf{R}$ alone is incomplete.
> 5. **Do not forget the principal point offset** $(c_x, c_y)$ in the intrinsic matrix — examiners explicitly test for this.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the equations of **perspective projection** for a pinhole camera using the similar-triangles principle. Clearly state the meaning of each variable. (7 marks)

**(b)** A point in the world has coordinates $\mathbf{P}_w = (2, 3, 5)^T$ m. The camera's extrinsic parameters are $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$ (identity) and $\mathbf{t} = (1, 1, 0)^T$ m. The intrinsic parameters are $f_x = 800$, $f_y = 800$, $c_x = 320$, $c_y = 240$, skew $s = 0$. Compute the pixel coordinates $(u, v)$ of the projected point. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**
1. Let $\mathbf{P}_c = (X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ be the camera-coordinate point.
2. Consider the line from $\mathbf{P}_c$ through the origin (CoP) to the image plane at $z = f$.
3. The line is parameterized as $\lambda \mathbf{P}_c$; it meets the image plane when $\lambda Z_c = f$, i.e., $\lambda = f / Z_c$.
4. The image-plane point is $(x, y, f) = (f X_c / Z_c, \, f Y_c / Z_c, \, f)$.
5. By similar triangles, the ratios $x / f = X_c / Z_c$ and $y / f = Y_c / Z_c$ hold.
6. Therefore: $\boxed{x = f X_c / Z_c, \quad y = f Y_c / Z_c}$.

**Model Solution for (b):**
Given $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$ and $\mathbf{t} = (1, 1, 0)^T$, the camera coordinates are:
$$
\mathbf{P}_c = \mathbf{R} \mathbf{P}_w + \mathbf{t} = (2 + 1, \, 3 + 1, \, 5 + 0)^T = (3, 4, 5)^T
$$

Depth $Z_c = 5$ m.

Using the intrinsic formula:
$$
u = f_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x = 800 \cdot \frac{3}{5} + 320 = 800 \cdot 0.6 + 320 = 480 + 320 = 800
$$

$$
v = f_y \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y = 800 \cdot \frac{4}{5} + 240 = 800 \cdot 0.8 + 240 = 640 + 240 = 880
$$

Therefore, the projected pixel coordinates are:
$$
\boxed{(u, v) = (800, 880)}
$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Define the **intrinsic camera matrix** $\mathbf{K}$. Derive its form starting from the metric image-plane coordinates. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain with diagrams the difference between **extrinsic** and **intrinsic** parameters. If $\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 1000 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and a 3D world point is $\mathbf{P}_w = (1, 0, 10)^T$ m, with $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$ and $\mathbf{t} = (0, 0, 0)^T$, find the pixel coordinates. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**
1. Metric image coordinates: $x = f X_c / Z_c$, $y = f Y_c / Z_c$.
2. Convert to pixel units: $u = x / \Delta u + c_x$, $v = y / \Delta v + c_y$.
3. Substitute: $u = (f / \Delta u) \cdot (X_c / Z_c) + c_x = f_x \cdot X_c / Z_c + c_x$.
4. Same for $v$ with $f_y = f / \Delta v$.
5. With skew $s$: $u = f_x \cdot X_c / Z_c + s \cdot Y_c / Z_c + c_x$.
6. Writing in homogeneous coordinates with $s = Z_c$:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}
$$

**Model Solution for (b):**
Since $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$ and $\mathbf{t} = (0,0,0)^T$, we have $\mathbf{P}_c = (1, 0, 10)^T$.
Then $Z_c = 10$ m.

$$
u = 1000 \cdot \frac{1}{10} + 320 = 100 + 320 = 420
$$

$$
v = 1000 \cdot \frac{0}{10} + 240 = 0 + 240 = 240
$$

Therefore, the pixel coordinates are:
$$
\boxed{(u, v) = (420, 240)}
$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Pinhole Camera Model**

- The **pinhole camera** is an idealization with a single point aperture; it produces an **inverted** image.
- The **focal length $f$** is the distance between the optical center and the image plane (in **mm** or **pixels**).
- The basic projection equations are $x = f X_c / Z_c$ and $y = f Y_c / Z_c$, derived from **similar triangles**.
- The mapping is **non-linear** because of the division by $Z_c$ — this is the geometric cause of **foreshortening**.
- The **intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$** (3×3) encodes internal camera properties: $f_x, f_y, s, c_x, c_y$ (5 DoF).
- The **extrinsic parameters** describe camera pose: rotation $\mathbf{R}$ (3 DoF) and translation $\mathbf{t}$ (3 DoF).
- Total DoF of the pinhole model = **11**.
- The full projection in homogeneous form is: $s [u, v, 1]^T = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \tilde{\mathbf{P}}_w$.
- The **perspective divide** (dividing by the third homogeneous coordinate) recovers the pixel location.
- **Depth $Z_c$** is lost during projection but controls the scale; two points on the same ray yield the same pixel.
- **Focal length in pixels**: $f_x = f / \Delta u$, $f_y = f / \Delta v$, where $\Delta u, \Delta v$ are pixel pitch in mm.
- **Principal point** $(c_x, c_y)$ is the pixel coordinate of the principal axis's intersection with the image plane, typically near $(W/2, H/2)$.
- **Skew parameter $s$** is zero for most modern cameras with rectangular pixels.
- Calibration algorithms (e.g., **Zhang's method**) recover $\mathbf{K}, \mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}$ using known 2D–3D correspondences.
- **Practical use in robotics:** Visual servoing, object pose estimation, SLAM, and structure-from-motion all build directly on this model.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Confusing focal length in mm with focal length in pixels; always convert using pixel pitch.
- **Memory trick:** "**P**inhole **P**rojects, **P**erspective **D**ivides" — remember the divide-by-$Z$ rule.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques for 3D reconstruction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT604 - SOFT COMPUTING AND COMPUTER VISION FOR ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Techniques for 3D reconstruction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 3 — Techniques for 3D Reconstruction

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **3D Reconstruction** is the process of capturing the shape and appearance of real-world objects or scenes from a set of 2D images (or other sensory data) and producing a computationally representable 3D model — typically as a point cloud, depth map, polygonal mesh, or volumetric grid — that can be used for navigation, manipulation, inspection, or rendering.

In robotics, 3D reconstruction is the *eyes-and-brain* pipeline that transforms flat pixels into a spatial understanding of the environment. It is the cornerstone of **autonomous navigation, SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), object manipulation, AR/VR, and digital twin creation**.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Eye Ruler" Intuition

Imagine you close one eye and try to touch the tip of a pen held in front of you. It is **hard** — your depth perception collapses. Now open both eyes. Suddenly, your brain fuses the two slightly different views (called **disparity**) and instantly knows *how far* the pen is.

> This is exactly how **stereo vision** reconstructs 3D — two cameras separated by a known distance (the **baseline**, **B**) act as the "two eyes," and the pixel shift between the two images (the **disparity**, **d**) is inversely proportional to the **depth (Z)**.

| Concept | Real-Eye Analogy | Robot Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Two viewpoints | Two human eyes | Stereo camera rig |
| Disparity | Retinal image difference | Pixel shift between cameras |
| Baseline | Inter-pupillary distance (~**6.3 cm**) | Distance between cameras |
| Focal length | Eye lens power | Camera focal length **f** |
| Depth perception | Stereopsis | Triangulation-based 3D reconstruction |

> [!NOTE]
> **3D Reconstruction ≠ 3D Scanning ≠ Depth Sensing**
> - **3D Reconstruction** is a *broad computational* pipeline that fuses geometry algorithms with image data.
> - **3D Scanning** is the physical/hardware act of capturing a 3D model.
> - **Depth Sensing** measures distance (per-pixel), but reconstruction *uses* depth to build surfaces/volumes.

### 🧭 Classification of 3D Reconstruction Techniques

3D reconstruction techniques can be broadly classified along three axes:

1. **By number of input views:**
   - **Monocular** (single view) → Shape-from-X, Mono-depth learning
   - **Binocular / Stereo** (two views) → Stereo triangulation
   - **Multi-view** (≥ 3 views) → SfM, MVS, Photometric stereo
2. **By sensing modality:**
   - **Passive (image-based)** — uses ambient light
   - **Active (projector/LiDAR/ToF-based)** — emits energy
3. **By output representation:**
   - Depth maps, point clouds, meshes, volumetric grids, implicit functions

> [!TIP]
> For the **KTU exam**, focus on: Stereo Vision, Structure from Motion, Shape from Shading, Shape from Focus/Defocus, Photometric Stereo, and Depth from Triangulation.

### 📐 Foundational Mathematical Objects (used across all techniques)

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **X, Y, Z** | 3D world coordinates | m / mm |
| **x, y** | 2D image coordinates | pixels |
| **f** | Focal length of the camera | mm or pixels |
| **B** | Baseline (distance between two cameras) | mm / m |
| **d** | Disparity (pixel shift between stereo pair) | pixels |
| **K** | Camera intrinsic matrix (3×3) | unitless |
| **[R | t]** | Camera extrinsic (rotation + translation) | rotation + m |
| **E, F** | Essential & Fundamental matrices | unitless |
| **n** | Surface normal vector | unitless |
| **I(x, y)** | Image intensity at pixel (x, y) | [0, 255] or normalized |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Big Picture — 3D Reconstruction Pipeline

Every 3D reconstruction system, regardless of technique, follows the same skeleton:

> **Step 1 → Image Acquisition** → **Step 2 → Camera Calibration** → **Step 3 → Feature Extraction** → **Step 4 → Correspondence Matching** → **Step 5 → Depth/Geometry Estimation** → **Step 6 → Surface/Volume Reconstruction** → **Step 7 → Post-processing & Refinement**

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus emphasizes **techniques**, so let us break down the **five foundational approaches**.

---

## 2.2 Technique 1: **Stereo Vision (Binocular Triangulation)**

### 🧠 Core Idea
Two cameras separated by a baseline **B** capture the same 3D point from slightly different angles. The pixel position of that point in the two images differs by a quantity called **disparity (d)**. Triangulating this disparity yields depth.

### 🪜 Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Rectify** both images so that corresponding points lie on the **same horizontal scanline** (epipolar lines become horizontal).
2. For each pixel $(x_L, y)$ in the **left image**, find the **matching pixel** $(x_R, y)$ in the **right image** using a similarity metric (SSD, SAD, NCC, or CNN-based descriptor).
3. Compute **disparity**:
   $$d = x_L - x_R$$
4. Use the **triangulation equation** to compute depth **Z**:
   $$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$
5. Reconstruct (X, Y) using the pinhole projection model:
   $$X = \frac{(x_L - c_x) \cdot Z}{f}, \quad Y = \frac{(y - c_y) \cdot Z}{f}$$

### 🌍 Real-World Utility
- Autonomous vehicles (obstacle detection)
- Robotic pick-and-place (bin picking)
- Surgical robotics (endoscopic depth)
- UAV obstacle avoidance

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $Z = \frac{B \cdot d}{f}$ (inverted). Always remember: **small disparity ⇒ large depth**; **large disparity ⇒ small depth**.

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet — Stereo Vision

| Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | Depth from disparity | m |
| $d = x_L - x_R$ | Disparity definition | pixels |
| $\delta Z = \frac{Z^2}{f \cdot B} \cdot \delta d$ | Depth uncertainty | m |
| $R = \frac{B \cdot f}{Z \cdot \Delta p}$ | Range resolution limit | m |
| $X = Z \cdot (x - c_x) / f$ | World X from image x | m |
| $Y = Z \cdot (y - c_y) / f$ | World Y from image y | m |

Where:
- $c_x, c_y$ = principal point coordinates
- $\Delta p$ = pixel size (sensor pitch)
- $\delta d$ = disparity quantization error (typically **0.5 pixel**)

---

## 2.3 Technique 2: **Structure from Motion (SfM)**

### 🧠 Core Idea
SfM recovers both the **3D structure of the scene** AND the **camera poses** simultaneously from a set of **2D images taken from different unknown viewpoints**. It is essentially stereo vision generalized to *N* cameras with unknown positions.

### 🪜 Pipeline

1. **Feature Detection** — SIFT, SURF, ORB on every image.
2. **Feature Matching** — Brute-force or FLANN matching across image pairs.
3. **Geometric Verification** — RANSAC estimation of the **Fundamental matrix F** between pairs.
4. **Pose Recovery** — Decompose **E = Kᵀ F K** to recover **R** and **t** (camera extrinsics).
5. **Triangulation** — Reconstruct sparse 3D points from 2D correspondences.
6. **Bundle Adjustment** — Globally refine all 3D points and camera poses by minimizing **reprojection error**:
   $$\min_{P_i, X_j} \sum_{i,j} \left\| \pi(P_i, X_j) - x_{ij} \right\|^2$$
   where $\pi(\cdot)$ is the camera projection function, $P_i$ is camera *i*'s pose, $X_j$ is 3D point *j*, and $x_{ij}$ is the observed pixel.

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet — SfM / Epipolar Geometry

| Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $x'^{\top} F x = 0$ | Epipolar constraint (Fundamental matrix) |
| $\hat{x}'^{\top} E \hat{x} = 0$ | Epipolar constraint (Essential matrix, normalized) |
| $E = [t]_\times R$ | Essential matrix from R, t |
| $F = K^{-\top} E K^{-1}$ | Fundamental from Essential |
| $P = K \cdot [R \mid t]$ | Camera projection matrix |
| $\min \sum \vert \pi(P_i, X_j) - x_{ij} \vert^2$ | Bundle Adjustment objective |

---

## 2.4 Technique 3: **Shape from Shading (SFS)**

### 🧠 Core Idea
A **single 2D image** of an object is enough to estimate its 3D shape — *if* we know the lighting direction and the surface's reflectance model. The brightness of each pixel encodes information about the **surface normal**.

### 🪜 Logic

1. Assume a **Lambertian** surface (brightness independent of viewing direction).
2. For a surface with normal **n** = $(n_x, n_y, n_z)$ and a point light source direction **s** = $(s_x, s_y, s_z)$, the **image irradiance equation** is:
   $$I(x, y) = \rho \cdot \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{s} = \rho \cdot (n_x s_x + n_y s_y + n_z s_z)$$
   where $\rho$ is the albedo.
3. The surface gradient $(p, q) = (\partial Z / \partial x, \partial Z / \partial y)$ relates to the normal:
   $$n = \frac{(-p, -q, 1)}{\sqrt{p^2 + q^2 + 1}}$$
4. Solve the resulting **Eikonal-type PDE** using methods like:
   - **Horn's iterative method**
   - **Lee & Kuo's linearization**
   - **Tsai & Shah's discrete approach**

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet — Shape from Shading

| Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $I = \rho \cdot \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{s}$ | Image irradiance (Lambertian) |
| $n = \frac{(-p, -q, 1)}{\sqrt{p^2 + q^2 + 1}}$ | Normal from depth gradient |
| $R(p, q) = \frac{1 + p p_s + q q_s}{\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2} \sqrt{1 + p_s^2 + q_s^2}}$ | Reflectance map |
| $I(x, y) = R(p, q)$ | SFS as PDE |

---

## 2.5 Technique 4: **Shape from Focus / Defocus (SFF / SFD)**

### 🧠 Core Idea
A camera lens can only focus at **one distance** at a time. Pixels of objects **in focus** are **sharp** (high gradient magnitude); pixels of objects **out of focus** are **blurred**. By varying the focus setting (or by exploiting the lens's depth-dependent PSF), we can estimate per-pixel depth.

### 🪜 Logic (SFF)

1. Capture a **focus volume** — a stack of $N$ images while moving the lens through different focus settings.
2. For each pixel $(x, y)$, compute a **focus measure** $F_n(x, y)$ for image $n$ (e.g., Laplacian, sum-modified-Laplacian, Tenenbaum gradient).
3. The depth at $(x, y)$ is the focus setting $d_n$ that **maximizes** the focus measure:
   $$Z(x, y) = \arg\max_n F_n(x, y)$$

### 🪜 Logic (SFD)

1. Capture **two images** of the same scene with different aperture or focal settings.
2. The **amount of blur (radius of circle of confusion $c$)** is related to depth:
   $$c = \frac{\vert s - f \vert}{s} \cdot \frac{f}{N_f} \cdot \frac{D - s_f}{D - f}$$
3. Estimate $c$ locally from relative blur between the two images.

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet — SFF / SFD

| Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $F(x, y) = \sum \vert \nabla^2 I(x, y) \vert$ | Laplacian focus measure |
| $Z(x, y) = \arg\max_d F_d(x, y)$ | SFF depth selection |
| $c = \frac{D \cdot f \cdot \vert s - f \vert}{f \cdot N_f \cdot (D - s)}$ | Circle of confusion |
| $\sigma \propto \frac{1}{D} \cdot \frac{\vert D - f \vert}{N_f}$ | Defocus blur kernel size |

---

## 2.6 Technique 5: **Photometric Stereo**

### 🧠 Core Idea
If we keep the **camera fixed** but vary the **illumination direction**, the *same* surface point produces different brightnesses. With **≥ 3 known light directions**, we can solve for the **surface normal** and **albedo** at every pixel — *pixel-wise 3D recovery*.

### 🪜 Logic

1. Capture $k \geq 3$ images of a static scene, each with a different known light direction $\mathbf{s}_i$.
2. For pixel $(x, y)$, the Lambertian model gives:
   $$I_i(x, y) = \rho(x, y) \cdot \mathbf{n}(x, y) \cdot \mathbf{s}_i$$
3. Stack $k$ equations as a matrix system:
   $$\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \\ \vdots \\ I_k \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} s_1^{\top} \\ s_2^{\top} \\ \vdots \\ s_k^{\top} \end{bmatrix} \cdot (\rho \mathbf{n})$$
4. Solve the linear least-squares system for the unknown vector $\mathbf{g} = \rho \mathbf{n} = (g_1, g_2, g_3)^{\top}$:
   $$\mathbf{g} = (S^{\top} S)^{-1} S^{\top} \mathbf{I}$$
5. Recover normal $\mathbf{n} = \mathbf{g} / \vert\vert \mathbf{g} \vert\vert$ and albedo $\rho = \vert\vert \mathbf{g} \vert\vert$.

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet — Photometric Stereo

| Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $I_i = \rho \cdot \mathbf{n}^{\top} \mathbf{s}_i$ | Lambertian photometric image |
| $\mathbf{g} = (S^{\top} S)^{-1} S^{\top} \mathbf{I}$ | Least-squares normal + albedo |
| $n = \mathbf{g} / \vert\vert \mathbf{g} \vert\vert$ | Unit normal |
| $\rho = \vert\vert \mathbf{g} \vert\vert$ | Albedo (reflectance) |
| $Z(x, y) = \iint (p, q) \, dx \, dy$ | Depth from normals (integration) |

---

## 2.7 Comparison Table — All Techniques at a Glance

| Technique | # Cameras | # Images | Lighting | Output | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stereo Vision | 2 | 2 pair | Ambient | Dense depth | Simple, fast | Textureless fail |
| Structure from Motion | ≥ 2 | ≥ 2 | Ambient | Sparse/dense 3D | Flexible, sparse-to-dense | Computationally heavy |
| Shape from Shading | 1 | 1 | Known | Fine surface detail | Single image | Ill-posed, requires light |
| Shape from Focus | 1 | $N$ stack | Ambient | Dense depth | Sub-mm accuracy | Slow, narrow DOF |
| Photometric Stereo | 1 | $k \geq 3$ | $k$ controlled | Normal + albedo | High detail | Needs calibrated lights |
| Multi-View Stereo | ≥ 2 | Many | Ambient | Dense 3D | High accuracy | Slow, large data |
| Structured Light | 1 + projector | 1+ | Active pattern | Dense depth | Robust to texture | Outdoor interference |
| Time-of-Flight | 1 (ToF cam) | 1 | Active IR | Real-time depth | Per-pixel depth | Low resolution, noise |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section derives the **two most exam-critical equations** in 3D reconstruction: the **Stereo Triangulation Depth Equation** and the **Photometric Stereo Normal Recovery** — fully, with every algebraic step.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Stereo Triangulation — $Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$

### 🧭 Geometric Setup

Consider two cameras with the **same focal length $f$**, separated by a **baseline $B$** along the **X-axis**. Their optical axes are parallel. A 3D point $\mathbf{P} = (X, Y, Z)$ projects to:
- Left image at $\mathbf{p}_L = (x_L, y_L)$
- Right image at $\mathbf{p}_R = (x_R, y_R)$

After **stereo rectification**, both points lie on the **same horizontal scanline** ($y_L = y_R = y$).

### 🪜 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Pinhole projection in the LEFT camera:**

Using similar triangles formed by the principal ray and the projected ray to point $P$:

$$
\frac{x_L}{f} = \frac{X}{Z}
$$

Solving for $X$:

$$
X = \frac{x_L \cdot Z}{f}
$$

**Step 2 — Pinhole projection in the RIGHT camera:**

The right camera's origin is shifted by $B$ along $X$. So the 3D point's $X$-coordinate *relative to the right camera* is $(X - B)$:

$$
\frac{x_R}{f} = \frac{X - B}{Z}
$$

Solving for $(X - B)$:

$$
X - B = \frac{x_R \cdot Z}{f}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the two equations:**

$$
X - (X - B) = \frac{x_L \cdot Z}{f} - \frac{x_R \cdot Z}{f}
$$

The left side simplifies to $B$:

$$
B = \frac{Z}{f} \cdot (x_L - x_R)
$$

**Step 4 — Define disparity:**

Disparity is the difference in pixel positions:

$$
d = x_L - x_R
$$

Substituting into Step 3:

$$
B = \frac{Z \cdot d}{f}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $Z$ (the depth):**

$$
Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}
$$

✅ **Final Result — Stereo Depth Equation.**

### 📊 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> **Problem:** A stereo camera has focal length $f = 50$ mm, baseline $B = 120$ mm. A 3D point projects to $x_L = 312$ pixels and $x_R = 287$ pixels in the rectified left/right images. The pixel size is $\Delta p = 0.01$ mm/pixel. Compute (a) disparity, (b) depth, (c) world X-coordinate.

**Step (a) — Disparity:**

$$
d = x_L - x_R = 312 - 287 = 25 \text{ pixels}
$$

**Step (b) — Depth (using pixel units of $f$):**

First convert $f$ to pixel units:

$$
f_{\text{px}} = \frac{f}{\Delta p} = \frac{50}{0.01} = 5000 \text{ pixels}
$$

Now apply the depth equation:

$$
Z = \frac{f_{\text{px}} \cdot B}{d} = \frac{5000 \cdot 120}{25} = 24000 \text{ mm} = 24 \text{ m}
$$

**Step (c) — World X-coordinate (assuming principal point $c_x = 320$ pixels):**

$$
X = \frac{(x_L - c_x) \cdot Z \cdot \Delta p}{f} = \frac{(312 - 320) \cdot 24{,}000 \cdot 0.01}{50}
$$

$$
X = \frac{(-8) \cdot 240}{50} = \frac{-1920}{50} = -38.4 \text{ mm}
$$

The negative sign indicates the point is to the **left** of the left camera's principal axis.

### 🔁 Depth Uncertainty Derivation (Bonus)

Differentiating $Z = fB/d$ with respect to $d$:

$$
\frac{\partial Z}{\partial d} = -\frac{fB}{d^2} = -\frac{Z^2}{fB}
$$

Taking magnitudes and substituting a typical disparity quantization error $\delta d$:

$$
\delta Z = \frac{Z^2}{fB} \cdot \delta d
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Depth uncertainty grows as **$Z^2$** — a 2× increase in distance leads to 4× worse depth precision. This is why stereo vision degrades rapidly for distant objects.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Photometric Stereo — Recovering Surface Normal

### 🧭 Setup

We have a **single fixed camera** viewing a **static surface**. We capture $k$ images, each with a *different known* light source direction $\mathbf{s}_i \in \mathbb{R}^3$ (with $\vert\vert \mathbf{s}_i \vert\vert = 1$). The surface has Lambertian reflectance with albedo $\rho(x, y)$ and unit normal $\mathbf{n}(x, y)$.

### 🪜 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Lambertian Image Formation:**

For image $i$, the brightness at pixel $(x, y)$ is:

$$
I_i(x, y) = \rho(x, y) \cdot \mathbf{n}(x, y)^{\top} \mathbf{s}_i
$$

(We drop $(x, y)$ for clarity, treating one pixel at a time.)

**Step 2 — Define the "generalized reflectance vector":**

Define $\mathbf{g} = \rho \cdot \mathbf{n}$. This is a 3D vector that simultaneously encodes **albedo (magnitude)** and **normal (direction)**. The image equation becomes:

$$
I_i = \mathbf{g}^{\top} \mathbf{s}_i = s_{i,1} g_1 + s_{i,2} g_2 + s_{i,3} g_3
$$

**Step 3 — Write the $k$ equations as a matrix system:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \\ \vdots \\ I_k \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} s_{1,1} & s_{1,2} & s_{1,3} \\ s_{2,1} & s_{2,2} & s_{2,3} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ s_{k,1} & s_{k,2} & s_{k,3} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} g_1 \\ g_2 \\ g_3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

In compact form:

$$
\mathbf{I} = S \cdot \mathbf{g}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the over-determined least-squares system (for $k > 3$):**

The normal equations give:

$$
S^{\top} \mathbf{I} = S^{\top} S \mathbf{g}
$$

$$
\mathbf{g} = (S^{\top} S)^{-1} S^{\top} \mathbf{I}
$$

**Step 5 — Recover normal and albedo separately:**

The unit normal is the direction of $\mathbf{g}$:

$$
\mathbf{n} = \frac{\mathbf{g}}{\vert\vert \mathbf{g} \vert\vert}
$$

The albedo is its magnitude:

$$
\rho = \vert\vert \mathbf{g} \vert\vert = \sqrt{g_1^2 + g_2^2 + g_3^2}
$$

✅ **Final Result — Photometric Stereo Recovery.**

### 📊 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> **Problem:** A photometric stereo system uses 3 light sources with directions:
> $\mathbf{s}_1 = (0, 0, 1)^{\top}$, $\mathbf{s}_2 = (1, 0, 1)/\sqrt{2}$, $\mathbf{s}_3 = (0, 1, 1)/\sqrt{2}$.
> The measured intensities at a pixel are $I_1 = 128$, $I_2 = 160$, $I_3 = 160$ (in 8-bit [0, 255] scale). Find the surface normal and albedo at this pixel.

**Step 1 — Form the light-direction matrix $S$:**

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 1/\sqrt{2} & 0 & 1/\sqrt{2} \\ 0 & 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0.7071 & 0 & 0.7071 \\ 0 & 0.7071 & 0.7071 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the measurement vector $\mathbf{I}$:**

$$
\mathbf{I} = \begin{bmatrix} 128 \\ 160 \\ 160 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $S^{\top} S$:**

$$
S^{\top} S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $(S^{\top} S)^{-1}$:**

Determinant and inverse calculation yields (verify by matrix inversion):

$$
(S^{\top} S)^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 3 & -1 \\ -1 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $S^{\top} \mathbf{I}$:**

$$
S^{\top} \mathbf{I} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \cdot 128 + 0.7071 \cdot 160 + 0 \cdot 160 \\ 0 \cdot 128 + 0 \cdot 160 + 0.7071 \cdot 160 \\ 1 \cdot 128 + 0.7071 \cdot 160 + 0.7071 \cdot 160 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 113.14 \\ 113.14 \\ 354.27 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\mathbf{g} = (S^{\top} S)^{-1} S^{\top} \mathbf{I}$:**

$$
\mathbf{g} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 3 & -1 \\ -1 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 113.14 \\ 113.14 \\ 354.27 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Row-wise:

$$
g_1 = 3(113.14) + 1(113.14) - 1(354.27) = 339.42 + 113.14 - 354.27 = 98.29
$$

$$
g_2 = 1(113.14) + 3(113.14) - 1(354.27) = 113.14 + 339.42 - 354.27 = 98.29
$$

$$
g_3 = -1(113.14) - 1(113.14) + 1(354.27) = -113.14 - 113.14 + 354.27 = 127.99
$$

**Step 7 — Compute albedo and normal:**

$$
\rho = \sqrt{98.29^2 + 98.29^2 + 127.99^2} = \sqrt{9660.93 + 9660.93 + 16381.44} = \sqrt{35703.3} \approx 188.95
$$

$$
\mathbf{n} = \frac{1}{188.95} \begin{bmatrix} 98.29 \\ 98.29 \\ 127.99 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.520 \\ 0.520 \\ 0.677 \end{bmatrix}
$$

✅ **Final Result:** Albedo $\rho \approx 189$, Normal $\mathbf{n} \approx (0.52, 0.52, 0.68)$.

> [!NOTE]
> Verification: $\vert\vert \mathbf{n} \vert\vert = \sqrt{0.52^2 + 0.52^2 + 0.68^2} = \sqrt{0.27 + 0.27 + 0.46} = \sqrt{1.00} = 1$ ✓ (unit normal confirmed).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Technique to Use?

| Scenario | Best Technique | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Real-time robot navigation, indoor, textured | Stereo Vision | Fast, robust with textures |
| Outdoor scene, no calibration | SfM | No camera calibration needed |
| Inspecting fine surface detail (defect detection) | Photometric Stereo | Sub-mm surface normal precision |
| Textureless / uniformly painted object | Shape from Focus / Photometric Stereo | Texture-independent |
| One photograph, no metadata | Shape from Shading | Single image works |
| Large-scale mapping (city, building) | Multi-View Stereo / SfM | Handles wide baselines |
| Bin picking in factory | Structured Light | Robust to featureless objects |
| Autonomous driving (night) | LiDAR / ToF / Stereo | Active sensing works in dark |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stereo Vision — Triangulation Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[3D Point P at depth Z] -->|ray 1| B[Left Camera focal f]
    A -->|ray 2| C[Right Camera focal f]
    B -->|projects to| D[pixel xL]
    C -->|projects to| E[pixel xR]
    D --> F[Disparity Calculator]
    E --> F
    F -->|d = xL - xR| G[Depth Z = fB / d]
    G --> H[3D Point Cloud]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Figure explanation:** Two cameras with parallel optical axes separated by baseline **B** view a 3D point **P** at depth **Z**. The same point projects to different pixel positions $x_L$ and $x_R$ in the two images. The horizontal pixel difference is the **disparity**, which feeds the depth formula.

---

## 4.2 Generic 3D Reconstruction Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Image Acquisition] --> S2[Step 2: Camera Calibration]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Feature Extraction SIFT ORB SURF]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Feature Matching and Correspondence]
    S4 --> S5{Step 5: Choose Technique}
    S5 -->|Stereo| T1[Binocular Triangulation]
    S5 -->|Multi View| T2[Structure from Motion]
    S5 -->|Active| T3[Structured Light or ToF]
    S5 -->|Single Image| T4[Shape from Shading or Focus]
    S5 -->|Multi Light| T5[Photometric Stereo]
    T1 --> S6[Step 6: Depth or 3D Point Recovery]
    T2 --> S6
    T3 --> S6
    T4 --> S6
    T5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Surface Mesh or Volumetric Fusion]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Post Processing Filtering and Texturing]
    S8 --> S9[Final 3D Model]

    style S1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333
    style S2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333
    style S3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333
    style S4 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333
```
