# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Define K-Means clustering. List any two of its limitations.

**Model Answer:**
K-Means is an unsupervised, prototype-based, hard clustering algorithm that partitions $N$ data points into $K$ disjoint clusters by minimizing the within-cluster sum of squared Euclidean distances $J = \sum_{n,k} r_{nk} \Vert \mathbf{x}_n - \mu_k \Vert^2$.

**Limitations (any two):**
1. Requires the number of clusters $K$ to be specified in advance.
2. Sensitive to initial centroid placement — can converge to a local (not global) minimum.
3. Assumes spherical, equally-sized clusters due to the use of Euclidean distance.
4. Highly sensitive to outliers because of the squared-distance objective.

*(Examiner expects 2 limitations for full 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Differentiate between supervised, unsupervised, and semi-supervised learning in the context of building a discriminant function.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised | Semi-supervised |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Labels** | All $N$ samples labeled | None labeled | Small labeled set + large unlabeled set |
| **Goal** | Learn $f: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ | Discover hidden structure / clusters | Improve supervised model using unlabeled data |
| **Discriminant** | From posteriors $P(C_i \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Hard/soft cluster ids from K-Means/GMM | Combined loss: supervised + regularization on manifold |
| **Examples** | LDA, SVM, KNN | K-Means, GMM, DBSCAN | Self-training, Transductive SVM |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, full-module questions allow internal choice. The model answers below are exhaustive for the "Question A / Question B" pair.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): K-Means Algorithm and EM for GMM**

**(a) [7 Marks]** State the K-Means objective function. Describe the **two iterative steps** of K-Means with the necessary equations. Show two iterations of K-Means on the dataset $\{(2,10), (2,6), (8,4), (5,8), (7,2), (6,4), (1,2), (4,9)\}$ with $K=2$ and initial centroids $\mu_1=(2,10)$ and $\mu_2=(5,8)$. State the converged cluster memberships.

**Model Solution:**

**K-Means objective:**
$$J = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} r_{nk}\, \Vert \mathbf{x}_n - \mu_k \Vert^2$$

**Step 1 — Assignment:**
$$r_{nk} = 1 \text{ if } k = \arg\min_j \Vert \mathbf{x}_n - \mu_j \Vert^2$$

**Step 2 — Refinement (centroid update):**
$$\mu_k = \frac{\sum_n r_{nk}\,\mathbf{x}_n}{\sum_n r_{nk}}$$

**Iteration 1 — Distances from $\mu_1=(2,10)$ and $\mu_2=(5,8)$:**

| Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2$ | Assigned |
|---|---|---|---|
| (2,10) | 0 | 13 | $C_1$ |
| (2,6)  | 16 | 13 | $C_2$ |
| (8,4)  | 72 | 25 | $C_2$ |
| (5,8)  | 18 | 0  | $C_2$ |
| (7,2)  | 89 | 40 | $C_2$ |
| (6,4)  | 52 | 17 | $C_2$ |
| (1,2)  | 65 | 52 | $C_2$ |
| (4,9)  | 5  | 2  | $C_2$ |

Cluster sizes: $|C_1| = 1$, $|C_2| = 7$.

**Centroid updates:**
$$\mu_1^{(1)} = (2,10), \qquad \mu_2^{(1)} = \left(\frac{2+8+5+7+6+1+4}{7},\; \frac{6+4+8+2+4+2+9}{7}\right) = (33/7,\; 35/7) = (4.71,\,5.00)$$

**Iteration 2 — Recompute distances using $\mu_1=(2,10)$, $\mu_2=(4.71,5.00)$:**

| Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2$ | Assigned |
|---|---|---|---|
| (2,10) | 0   | 31.4 | $C_1$ |
| (2,6)  | 16  | 7.3  | $C_2$ |
| (8,4)  | 72  | 11.1 | $C_2$ |
| (5,8)  | 18  | 9.1  | $C_2$ |
| (7,2)  | 89  | 11.1 | $C_2$ |
| (6,4)  | 52  | 2.1  | $C_2$ |
| (1,2)  | 65  | 17.6 | $C_2$ |
| (4,9)  | 5   | 16.2 | $C_1$ |

Cluster sizes: $|C_1| = 2$, $|C_2| = 6$.

**Centroid updates:**
$$\mu_1^{(2)} = \left(\frac{2+4}{2},\frac{10+9}{2}\right) = (3.0,\,9.5), \qquad \mu_2^{(2)} = \left(\frac{2+8+5+7+6+1}{6},\,\frac{6+4+8+2+4+2}{6}\right) = (29/6,\,26/6) = (4.83,\,4.33)$$

**Convergence (Iteration 3 — verify):** Recomputing distances with the new centroids does not change any assignment. The algorithm has converged.

**Final clusters:** $C_1 = \{(2,10), (4,9)\}$ with centroid $(3.0,\,9.5)$; $C_2 = \{(2,6), (8,4), (5,8), (7,2), (6,4), (1,2)\}$ with centroid $(4.83,\,4.33)$.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to the **Mixture of Gaussians (GMM)**, derive the **E-step** responsibility formula and the **M-step** update for the mean $\mu_k$ using the expected complete-data log-likelihood.

**Model Solution:**

The GMM density is:
$$p(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x} \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k), \quad \sum_k \pi_k = 1$$

**E-step — Posterior of latent variable (responsibility):** Using Bayes' rule, the posterior probability that component $k$ generated $\mathbf{x}_n$ is:

$$\gamma(z_{nk}) = p(z_k=1 \mid \mathbf{x}_n) = \frac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}$$

This follows directly from:
$$\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{p(z_k=1)\,p(\mathbf{x}_n \mid z_k=1)}{p(\mathbf{x}_n)} = \frac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_j \pi_j \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}$$

**M-step — Update for $\mu_k$:** Maximize the expected complete-data log-likelihood:

$$\mathcal{Q}(\theta,\theta^{\text{old}}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \big[\ln \pi_k + \ln \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)\big]$$

Expanding the log-Gaussian and dropping class-independent constants:

$$\mathcal{Q} \propto \sum_{n,k} \gamma(z_{nk}) \left[ -\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}_n - \mu_k)^{T}\Sigma_k^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_n - \mu_k) \right]$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $\mu_k$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{Q}}{\partial \mu_k} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \Sigma_k^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_n - \mu_k) = 0$$

Multiplying by $\Sigma_k$ and solving:

$$\mu_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_n \gamma(z_{nk})\,\mathbf{x}_n}{\sum_n \gamma(z_{nk})} = \frac{1}{N_k}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\gamma(z_{nk})\,\mathbf{x}_n \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Discriminant Function & Learning Paradigms**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define a **discriminant function** $g_i(\mathbf{x})$. Starting from the Bayesian classifier, derive the form of the discriminant function for two classes $C_1, C_2$ with **Gaussian class-conditional densities sharing a common covariance matrix $\Sigma$**. Show that the decision boundary is a **hyperplane**.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A discriminant function $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ assigns a scalar score to class $C_i$ for input $\mathbf{x}$. A new sample is classified as $C_i$ if $g_i(\mathbf{x}) > g_j(\mathbf{x})$ for all $j \neq i$.

**Bayesian classifier:** $\hat{y} = \arg\max_i P(C_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_i p(\mathbf{x} \mid C_i) P(C_i)$ (denominator $p(\mathbf{x})$ is class-independent).

**Gaussian class-conditional with shared $\Sigma$:**
$$p(\mathbf{x} \mid C_i) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\mu_i)^{T}\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\mu_i)\right)$$

**Log-discriminant:**
$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid C_i) + \ln P(C_i) = -\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\mu_i)^{T}\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\mu_i) - \frac{d}{2}\ln 2\pi - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma \vert + \ln P(C_i)$$

Drop the constants $\frac{d}{2}\ln 2\pi + \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma \vert$ (do not depend on $i$):

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\mu_i)^{T}\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\mu_i) + \ln P(C_i)$$

Expand the quadratic:
$$(\mathbf{x}-\mu_i)^{T}\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\mu_i) = \mathbf{x}^{T}\Sigma^{-1}\mathbf{x} - 2\mu_i^{T}\Sigma^{-1}\mathbf{x} + \mu_i^{T}\Sigma^{-1}\mu_i$$

The $\mathbf{x}^{T}\Sigma^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ term is class-independent and cancels. Define:

$$\mathbf{w}_i = \Sigma^{-1}\mu_i, \qquad w_{i0} = -\frac{1}{2}\mu_i^{T}\Sigma^{-1}\mu_i + \ln P(C_i)$$

Then:
$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_i^{T}\mathbf{x} + w_{i0}$$

**Decision boundary $g_1(\mathbf{x}) = g_2(\mathbf{x})$:**
$$(\mathbf{w}_1 - \mathbf{w}_2)^{T}\mathbf{x} = -(w_{10} - w_{20})$$

This is **linear in $\mathbf{x}$** — a hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^d$ with normal vector $\mathbf{w} = \Sigma^{-1}(\mu_1 - \mu_2)$. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **supervised, unsupervised, and semi-supervised learning** for building a discriminant function. Give **two real-world robotic/vision examples** for each paradigm.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison:**

| Property | Supervised | Unsupervised | Semi-supervised |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Label availability** | Every training sample labeled | Zero labels | Small labeled subset $L$ + large unlabeled subset $U$ |
| **Data requirement** | Expensive annotations needed | Cheap (raw data) | Cheap + selective annotation |
| **Output type** | Predictive classifier / regressor | Cluster ids / latent factors | Classifier refined by $U$ |
| **Typical objective** | Minimize loss on labeled set | Maximize likelihood / minimize distortion | Loss on $L$ + manifold / consistency regularizer on $U$ |
| **Generalization** | Limited by labeled diversity | Limited by cluster validity assumptions | Often better than supervised alone |

**Real-world examples:**

**Supervised:**
1. Robot grasping classifier trained on thousands of labeled grasp-success images.
2. Pedestrian detection using HOG + Linear SVM trained on labeled INRIA dataset.

**Unsupervised:**
1. K-Means clustering of LiDAR point clouds for obstacle segmentation in autonomous vehicles.
2. GMM-based skin-color model for face detection in surveillance robots.

**Semi-supervised:**
1. Autonomous driving — training a lane-detector on a few hand-labeled highway frames + millions of unlabelled road videos (self-training).
2. Medical image diagnosis — training a tumor classifier with a handful of expert-labeled CT scans + abundant unlabelled hospital archives.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Confusing hard and soft assignments** — K-Means produces *binary* $r_{nk}$; GMM produces *probabilistic* $\gamma(z_{nk})$. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the normalization constraint** $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$ in GMM derivations. Always state the constraint before applying the Lagrange multiplier.
> 3. **Skipping intermediate iteration steps** in numerical K-Means problems. KTU expects **at least 2 full iterations** with explicit distance tables, not just the final answer.
> 4. **Writing LDA weight vector incorrectly** — the form $\mathbf{w}_i = \Sigma^{-1}\mu_i$ applies *only* when $\Sigma$ is shared across classes. For per-class covariance (QDA), the discriminant becomes quadratic and $\Sigma^{-1}$ cannot be factored out.
> 5. **Confusing "argmax" with "max"** — the classifier decision is $\arg\max_i g_i(\mathbf{x})$, not $\max_i g_i(\mathbf{x})$. A common single-mark deduction.
> 6. **Forgetting to justify the EM convergence guarantee** — always mention that EM monotonically increases the log-likelihood and converges to a *local* (not global) maximum.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- **K-Means is unsupervised, hard, prototype-based** clustering with objective $J = \sum_{n,k} r_{nk} \Vert \mathbf{x}_n - \mu_k \Vert^2$.
- **Two steps of K-Means:** (1) assign each point to the *nearest* centroid; (2) recompute each centroid as the *mean* of its assigned points.
- **K-Means converges** in finite steps to a *local minimum*; multiple restarts (or **K-Means++**) are recommended.
- **K-Means complexity** is $\mathcal{O}(NKdI)$ — linear in $N$, suitable for large datasets.
- **GMM** is a probabilistic model: $p(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_k \pi_k \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x} \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$ with constraints $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$, $\pi_k \geq 0$.
- **GMM ↔ K-Means:** K-Means is the limit of GMM when $\Sigma_k = \epsilon \mathbf{I}$ and $\epsilon \to 0$.
- **E-step** computes responsibilities $\gamma(z_{nk})$ via Bayes' rule — a soft assignment in $[0,1]$.
- **M-step** updates $\mu_k = \frac{1}{N_k}\sum_n \gamma(z_{nk})\mathbf{x}_n$, $\Sigma_k = \frac{1}{N_k}\sum_n \gamma(z_{nk})(\mathbf{x}_n-\mu_k)(\mathbf{x}_n-\mu_k)^{T}$, $\pi_k = N_k/N$.
- **EM monotonically increases log-likelihood** at each iteration and converges to a *local* maximum.
- **Discriminant function:** $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ assigns a score per class; classify via $\arg\max_i g_i(\mathbf{x})$.
- **Bayesian form:** $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid C_i) + \ln P(C_i)$ (log of posterior is class-equivalent).
- **LDA form** (shared $\Sigma$): $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_i^{T}\mathbf{x} + w_{i0}$ with $\mathbf{w}_i = \Sigma^{-1}\mu_i$, $w_{i0} = -\frac{1}{2}\mu_i^{T}\Sigma^{-1}\mu_i + \ln P(C_i)$.
- **LDA decision boundary is a hyperplane**; **QDA** (distinct $\Sigma_i$) gives a **quadric surface**.
- **Supervised learning** uses *all* labeled data; **unsupervised** uses *no* labels; **semi-supervised** uses *few labels + many unlabeled* and exploits the data distribution.
- **Silhouette score** $s(n) = \frac{b(n) - a(n)}{\max\{a(n), b(n)\}}$ is a standard metric for choosing $K$ in K-Means.
- **BIC / AIC** are model-selection criteria commonly used to choose the number of GMM components.
- **Real-world hooks to memorize for viva:** K-Means → BoVW codebook; GMM → skin-color / background subtraction; Semi-supervised → medical imaging / autonomous driving.
- **Examiner's mantra:** Always state assumptions, name the objective, write the update, show convergence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classifiers: Bayes, KNN, ANN models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT604 - SOFT COMPUTING AND COMPUTER VISION FOR ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classifiers: Bayes, KNN, ANN models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Big Picture: What is a Classifier?

In the context of **Soft Computing and Computer Vision for Robots**, a **classifier** is a supervised machine learning model that learns a mapping from an input feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ to a discrete output class label $y \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$. For a robot, this means deciding *what* it is seeing (object recognition), *where* it is (scene classification), or *how* to act (behavior classification).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The three canonical classifier families tested in this module are **Bayes Classifiers (Probabilistic)**, **K-Nearest Neighbors (Instance-Based / Lazy Learning)**, and **Artificial Neural Networks (Function Approximators)**. These represent three fundamentally different inductive biases in machine learning.

---

### 1.2 Bayes Classifier — The Statistician's Approach

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** A Bayes classifier is a probabilistic model that predicts the class label $\hat{y}$ that maximizes the posterior probability $P(y \mid \mathbf{x})$ using **Bayes' Theorem**.

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} P(y \mid \mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_{y} \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid y) P(y)}{P(\mathbf{x})}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Medical Diagnosis Intuition):** Imagine a doctor diagnosing whether a robot has a faulty sensor. The doctor asks: "Given that the temperature reading is 90°C (evidence), what is the probability the sensor is broken (hypothesis)?" This is **posterior probability**. The doctor combines two things: (1) **Prior** — how often sensors generally fail, and (2) **Likelihood** — given a real failure, how likely is the temperature to hit 90°C. The Bayes classifier does exactly this — it inverts a *causal* prior to compute a *diagnostic* posterior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Naive Bayes Assumption:** Features $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ are conditionally independent given the class $y$. This "naive" simplification makes computation tractable:
> $$P(\mathbf{x} \mid y) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid y)$$
> This assumption is rarely strictly true (e.g., pixel intensities in an image are correlated) but produces surprisingly robust results in vision pipelines.

---

### 1.3 K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) — The Memory-Based Approach

**Formal Definition:** KNN is a **non-parametric, instance-based** learning algorithm that classifies a query point $\mathbf{x}_q$ by a majority vote of its **K closest training samples** in the feature space, using a chosen distance metric $d(\cdot, \cdot)$.

$$
\hat{y} = \mathrm{mode}\left(\{ y_i : \mathbf{x}_i \in N_K(\mathbf{x}_q) \} \right)
$$

where $N_K(\mathbf{x}_q)$ denotes the set of K training points nearest to $\mathbf{x}_q$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Neighborhood Watch Intuition):** A new resident moves into a neighborhood and wants to know the political leaning of the street. Instead of asking the whole town, they just ask their **5 nearest neighbors**. If 3 are liberal and 2 are conservative, the new resident is classified as living in a "liberal" area. KNN does precisely this — it predicts labels based on the *local geometry* of the feature space. It is also called a **lazy learner** because it does almost no work during training; all computation is deferred to query time.

---

### 1.4 Artificial Neural Network (ANN) — The Brain-Inspired Approach

**Formal Definition:** An ANN is a **parametric function approximator** composed of interconnected computational units (neurons/artificial perceptrons) organized in layers, trained via **gradient-based optimization** (typically backpropagation) to learn an input-output mapping $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^K$.

A single artificial neuron computes:
$$
z = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b, \quad a = \sigma(z)
$$

where $\mathbf{w}$ is the weight vector, $b$ is the bias, and $\sigma$ is a non-linear **activation function**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Assembly Line Intuition):** Imagine a robotic assembly line. Raw materials (input features $\mathbf{x}$) enter Station 1 (Layer 1), where workers (neurons) partially assemble them and pass the result to Station 2 (Layer 2), and so on, until a finished product (class probability) emerges. Each worker has a personal "skill rating" (**weight**) and a "default error" (**bias**). Through thousands of trials and supervisor feedback (**backpropagation**), each worker adjusts their skills to reduce defects (**loss**). The deep hierarchical processing is what makes ANNs the powerhouse behind modern computer vision (e.g., CNNs for object detection in robots).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why All Three?** KTU 2024 emphasizes that no single classifier is universally best. Bayes classifiers excel when probabilistic reasoning is needed; KNN shines for small datasets with low-dimensional features; ANNs dominate when abundant data and complex non-linear patterns exist (typical in robot vision). Understanding their **inductive biases** is critical for engineering selection in robotic pipelines.

---

### 1.5 Comparative Summary at a Glance

| Property | Bayes (Naive) | KNN | ANN |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Learning Type** | Generative (probabilistic) | Lazy (instance-based) | Discriminative (parametric) |
| **Training Cost** | Low (count statistics) | Zero (stores data) | High (iterative optimization) |
| **Query Cost** | Low | High ($O(Nd)$) | Low (single forward pass) |
| **Parametric?** | No (assumes distribution) | No (non-parametric) | Yes (weights & biases) |
| **Handles Non-linearity** | Limited (linear decision boundary) | Yes (locally adaptive) | Yes (universal approximator) |
| **Data Hungry?** | Low | Medium | Very High |
| **Interpretability** | High | Medium | Low (black box) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bayes Classifier — Operational Logic

The Bayes decision rule assigns a query to the class with the **maximum posterior**. The derivation chain is:

**Step 1 — Prior Probability:**
$$P(y) = \frac{\text{Number of samples in class } y}{\text{Total number of samples}}$$

**Step 2 — Likelihood (Class-Conditional Density):**
For continuous features, often assumed **Gaussian**:
$$P(x_i \mid y) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{y}^{2}}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu_y)^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Evidence (Normalization Constant):**
$$P(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{y} P(\mathbf{x} \mid y) P(y)$$

**Step 4 — Posterior (Bayes' Theorem):**
$$P(y \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid y) P(y)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Step 5 — Decision Rule (MAP — Maximum A Posteriori):**
$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} P(y \mid \mathbf{x}) \equiv \arg\max_{y} P(\mathbf{x} \mid y) P(y)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Naive" assumption works in robotics:** Even though pixel intensities are not independent, the **zero-frequency problem** is avoided (smoothing with Laplace/add-one), and the model trains from tiny datasets. For a robot with a 5-class color classifier using RGB histograms, Naive Bayes often outperforms more complex models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Boundary:** The Bayes classifier with Gaussian assumptions produces a **quadratic decision boundary** in 2D. With shared covariance across classes, it degenerates to a **linear boundary** (Linear Discriminant Analysis — LDA). The "naive" diagonal covariance assumption gives a special case of QDA with axis-aligned ellipses.

---

### 2.2 KNN — Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Choose Distance Metric $d(\cdot, \cdot)$:** Common choices in robotic vision:
- **Euclidean:** $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n}(x_{ik} - x_{jk})^2}$
- **Manhattan:** $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert$
- **Minkowski (generalized):** $d_p(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \left(\sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$

**Step 2 — Choose K:** Small K → noisy, high variance. Large K → biased, smoothed boundary. KTU expects students to know the **bias-variance tradeoff**.

**Step 3 — Find K Nearest Neighbors:** Sort all training points by distance to query.

**Step 4 — Majority Voting:**
$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in N_K(\mathbf{x}_q)} \mathbb{1}(y_i = c)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weighted KNN (KTU Expectation):** For better robustness, weight neighbors by inverse distance:
> $$w_i = \frac{1}{d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i) + \epsilon}$$
> $$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in N_K} w_i \cdot \mathbb{1}(y_i = c)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Curse of Dimensionality:** KNN degrades exponentially as feature dimension $n$ grows. In a unit hypercube, the ratio of the distance to nearest neighbor vs. farthest neighbor approaches **1.0** as $n \to \infty$ (Beyer et al., 1999). For robot vision, this means KNN is suitable for low-dimensional feature spaces (e.g., color histograms of 16-64 bins) but unsuitable for raw pixels in modern vision tasks.

---

### 2.3 Artificial Neural Network — Operational Logic

A **Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)** with one hidden layer computes:
$$
\mathbf{h} = \sigma_1(\mathbf{W}_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1), \quad \hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_2 \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}_2)
$$

**Step 1 — Forward Propagation:** Compute activations layer by layer.

**Step 2 — Loss Computation:** For classification, **Cross-Entropy Loss**:
$$\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{c=1}^{K} y_{ic} \log(\hat{y}_{ic})$$

**Step 3 — Backpropagation:** Apply the chain rule to compute gradients $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_\ell}$ for every layer $\ell$.

**Step 4 — Weight Update (Gradient Descent):**
$$\mathbf{W}_\ell^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{W}_\ell^{(t)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_\ell}$$
where $\eta$ is the **learning rate**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Activation Functions (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Sigmoid:** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$, range $(0,1)$, suffers from vanishing gradient.
> - **Tanh:** $\sigma(z) = \tanh(z) = \frac{e^z - e^{-z}}{e^z + e^{-z}}$, range $(-1,1)$, zero-centered.
> - **ReLU:** $\sigma(z) = \max(0, z)$, computationally efficient, default in modern vision networks.
> - **Softmax (output):** $\sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$, converts logits to probabilities summing to 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Approximation Theorem:** A feedforward MLP with a single hidden layer containing a finite number of neurons can approximate *any continuous function* on compact subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$ to arbitrary accuracy, given a non-linear activation (Hornik et al., 1989). This is the theoretical foundation for ANNs as universal classifiers in robotic perception.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET: The Big Five Equations You MUST Memorize**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(y \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid y) P(y)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | $P(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ always |
| 2 | Naive Bayes Likelihood | $P(\mathbf{x} \mid y) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid y)$ | Conditional independence assumed |
| 3 | Gaussian Likelihood | $P(x_i \mid y) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_y^2}}\exp\left(-\frac{(x_i-\mu_y)^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)$ | $\sigma_y^2 > 0$ |
| 4 | Euclidean Distance (KNN) | $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n}(x_{ik}-x_{jk})^2}$ | Minkowski $p=2$ |
| 5 | ANN Neuron Output | $a = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b)$ | $\sigma$ non-linear |
| 6 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{c=1}^{K} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ | $y_c$ one-hot encoded |
| 7 | Gradient Descent Update | $\mathbf{W} \leftarrow \mathbf{W} - \eta \nabla_{\mathbf{W}}\mathcal{L}$ | $\eta \in (0, 1)$ |
| 8 | Softmax | $\sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ | $\sum_i \sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = 1$ |
| 9 | ReLU Activation | $\sigma(z) = \max(0, z)$ | Non-saturating, sparse |
| 10 | Sigmoid Activation | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Vanishing gradient for $\vert z \vert > 4$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility in Robotics:**
> - **Bayes**: Spam-filter-like filtering of false sensor readings (Naive Bayes on thermal/visual cues).
> - **KNN**: Real-time object recognition on low-power edge devices with small reference libraries (e.g., classifying 10 known tools on a robot's workbench).
> - **ANN**: End-to-end perception stacks — object detection, semantic segmentation, grasp prediction, and reinforcement-learning-based control policies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Bayes Classifier Posterior Computation (Worked Numerical)

**Problem:** A robot uses a Naive Bayes classifier to distinguish between two obstacle types based on two sensor features: $x_1$ (proximity reading, normalized) and $x_2$ (surface texture coefficient). Training data yields:

- Class A (Wall): $P(A) = 0.6$, $\mu_{A,1} = 0.4, \sigma_{A,1}^2 = 0.05$, $\mu_{A,2} = 0.7, \sigma_{A,2}^2 = 0.10$
- Class B (Human): $P(B) = 0.4$, $\mu_{B,1} = 0.8, \sigma_{B,1}^2 = 0.04$, $\mu_{B,2} = 0.3, \sigma_{B,2}^2 = 0.08$

A new reading is $\mathbf{x} = (0.55, 0.45)$. Classify using MAP rule.

**Step 1 — Compute Gaussian likelihoods for $x_1$:**

For Class A:
$$
P(x_1=0.55 \mid A) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 0.05}} \exp\left(-\frac{(0.55-0.4)^2}{2 \cdot 0.05}\right)
$$

Numerator of exponent: $(0.55 - 0.4)^2 = (0.15)^2 = 0.0225$.
Exponent term: $-\frac{0.0225}{0.10} = -0.225$.
$\exp(-0.225) \approx 0.7985$.
Normalization: $\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 0.05} = \sqrt{0.3142} \approx 0.5606$.

$$
P(x_1=0.55 \mid A) = \frac{0.7985}{0.5606} \approx 1.4243
$$

For Class B:
$$
P(x_1=0.55 \mid B) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 0.04}} \exp\left(-\frac{(0.55-0.8)^2}{2 \cdot 0.04}\right)
$$

Numerator: $(0.55 - 0.8)^2 = (-0.25)^2 = 0.0625$.
Exponent: $-\frac{0.0625}{0.08} = -0.7813$.
$\exp(-0.7813) \approx 0.4578$.
Normalization: $\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 0.04} = \sqrt{0.2513} \approx 0.5013$.

$$
P(x_1=0.55 \mid B) = \frac{0.4578}{0.5013} \approx 0.9132
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Gaussian likelihoods for $x_2$:**

For Class A:
$$
P(x_2=0.45 \mid A) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 0.10}} \exp\left(-\frac{(0.45-0.7)^2}{2 \cdot 0.10}\right)
$$

Numerator: $(0.45-0.7)^2 = 0.0625$.
Exponent: $-\frac{0.0625}{0.20} = -0.3125$.
$\exp(-0.3125) \approx 0.7316$.
Normalization: $\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 0.10} = \sqrt{0.6283} \approx 0.7927$.

$$
P(x_2=0.45 \mid A) = \frac{0.7316}{0.7927} \approx 0.9229
$$

For Class B:
$$
P(x_2=0.45 \mid B) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 0.08}} \exp\left(-\frac{(0.45-0.3)^2}{2 \cdot 0.08}\right)
$$

Numerator: $(0.45-0.3)^2 = 0.0225$.
Exponent: $-\frac{0.0225}{0.16} = -0.1406$.
$\exp(-0.1406) \approx 0.8688$.
Normalization: $\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 0.08} = \sqrt{0.5027} \approx 0.7090$.

$$
P(x_2=0.45 \mid B) = \frac{0.8688}{0.7090} \approx 1.2254
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Naive Bayes product rule (independence):**

$$
P(\mathbf{x} \mid A) = P(x_1 \mid A) \cdot P(x_2 \mid A) = 1.4243 \times 0.9229 \approx 1.3145
$$

$$
P(\mathbf{x} \mid B) = P(x_1 \mid B) \cdot P(x_2 \mid B) = 0.9132 \times 1.2254 \approx 1.1190
$$

**Step 4 — Compute unnormalized posteriors (numerator of Bayes):**

$$
P(\mathbf{x} \mid A) P(A) = 1.3145 \times 0.6 = 0.7887
$$

$$
P(\mathbf{x} \mid B) P(B) = 1.1190 \times 0.4 = 0.4476
$$

**Step 5 — Decision (MAP rule):**

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max\{0.7887, 0.4476\} = A \;\; (\text{Wall})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The robot classifies the obstacle as a **Wall** with relative posterior weight $0.7887 / (0.7887 + 0.4476) \approx 0.638$ (i.e., 63.8% posterior probability for Wall). The MAP decision is made without explicitly computing $P(\mathbf{x})$ because it is a common normalization that cancels in $\arg\max$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: KNN Classification on a Toy 2D Dataset

**Problem:** Training set in 2D feature space (robot terrain difficulty: $y \in \{\text{Easy}, \text{Hard}\}$):

| Point | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 1 | 2 | Easy |
| $P_2$ | 2 | 3 | Easy |
| $P_3$ | 3 | 1 | Hard |
| $P_4$ | 4 | 4 | Hard |
| $P_5$ | 5 | 2 | Hard |

Classify the query point $\mathbf{q} = (2.5, 2.5)$ using **K = 3, Euclidean distance**.

**Step 1 — Compute Euclidean distance from $\mathbf{q}$ to each training point:**

Distance formula: $d(\mathbf{q}, P_i) = \sqrt{(q_1 - x_{i1})^2 + (q_2 - x_{i2})^2}$

$$
d(\mathbf{q}, P_1) = \sqrt{(2.5-1)^2 + (2.5-2)^2} = \sqrt{2.25 + 0.25} = \sqrt{2.5} \approx 1.581
$$

$$
d(\mathbf{q}, P_2) = \sqrt{(2.5-2)^2 + (2.5-3)^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.25} = \sqrt{0.5} \approx 0.707
$$

$$
d(\mathbf{q}, P_3) = \sqrt{(2.5-3)^2 + (2.5-1)^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 2.25} = \sqrt{2.5} \approx 1.581
$$

$$
d(\mathbf{q}, P_4) = \sqrt{(2.5-4)^2 + (2.5-4)^2} = \sqrt{2.25 + 2.25} = \sqrt{4.5} \approx 2.121
$$

$$
d(\mathbf{q}, P_5) = \sqrt{(2.5-5)^2 + (2.5-2)^2} = \sqrt{6.25 + 0.25} = \sqrt{6.5} \approx 2.550
$$

**Step 2 — Sort by distance (ascending) and select K=3 nearest:**

1. $P_2$ — Easy — $d \approx 0.707$
2. $P_1$ — Easy — $d \approx 1.581$
3. $P_3$ — Hard — $d \approx 1.581$
4. $P_4$ — Hard — $d \approx 2.121$
5. $P_5$ — Hard — $d \approx 2.550$

The **3 nearest** are $\{P_2, P_1, P_3\}$.

**Step 3 — Majority vote:**

Count: **Easy = 2, Hard = 1**. The majority class is **Easy**.

$$
\hat{y} = \mathrm{Easy}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on ties:** $P_1$ and $P_3$ are equidistant from $\mathbf{q}$. In practice, ties at the K-th boundary are broken by expanding K by 1 (use K=4) or by weighted voting. KTU questions may deliberately create such tie scenarios to test boundary awareness.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Single ANN Neuron Forward Pass + Gradient

**Problem:** A single neuron has weights $\mathbf{w} = (0.3, -0.5)^T$, bias $b = 0.1$, and uses **sigmoid** activation. Input $\mathbf{x} = (1.0, 0.8)^T$. Compute (a) the output $a$, and (b) the gradient $\frac{\partial a}{\partial w_1}$ assuming a target $y = 1$ and squared-error loss $L = \frac{1}{2}(y-a)^2$.

**Step 1 — Linear combination (pre-activation):**
$$
z = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = (0.3)(1.0) + (-0.5)(0.8) + 0.1 = 0.3 - 0.4 + 0.1 = 0.0
$$

**Step 2 — Sigmoid activation:**
$$
a = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}} = \frac{1}{1+e^{0}} = \frac{1}{1+1} = 0.5
$$

**Step 3 — Loss (squared error):**
$$
L = \frac{1}{2}(y-a)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 0.5)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.25) = 0.125
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1}$ via chain rule:**

First, $\frac{\partial a}{\partial z} = a(1-a)$ (sigmoid derivative). At $z=0$, $a=0.5$, so $\frac{\partial a}{\partial z} = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25$.

Next, $\frac{\partial z}{\partial w_1} = x_1 = 1.0$.

And $\frac{\partial L}{\partial a} = (a - y) = (0.5 - 1.0) = -0.5$.

By chain rule:
$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial a} \cdot \frac{\partial a}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial w_1} = (-0.5) \cdot (0.25) \cdot (1.0) = -0.125
$$

**Step 5 — Weight update with learning rate $\eta = 0.1$:**
$$
w_1^{\text{new}} = w_1 - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} = 0.3 - (0.1)(-0.125) = 0.3 + 0.0125 = 0.3125
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The negative gradient tells us the loss *decreases* if $w_1$ *increases*. The neuron "learns" to push the output $a$ closer to the target $y=1$ by strengthening the positive contribution of $x_1$. This is the essence of **backpropagation** in a single neuron; for an MLP, the same chain rule extends across layers.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Selection for Robotic Tasks

| Robot Task | Feature Type | Data Size | Best Classifier | Why |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tool identification (5 types) | Color histogram (16 bins) | Small (< 100) | KNN or Bayes | Low-dim, tiny dataset, fast inference |
| Terrain classification (RGB image patches) | CNN features (512-d) | Medium (10K) | ANN (MLP) | High-dim non-linear, need generalization |
| Fault detection in sensor stream | Single time series | Small + imbalanced | Naive Bayes | Probabilistic, interpretable thresholds |
| Real-time face detection | HOG features (few thousand dim) | Large (100K+) | ANN (Deep CNN) | High-dim, abundant data, non-linear |
| Voice command recognition | MFCC (13-39 dim) | Medium | KNN or ANN | Sequence-aware, but KNN competitive at low K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In real KTU lab projects (PBRUT604), students often build a simple 3-class image classifier (e.g., "red cup, blue cup, green cup") and are expected to **justify their choice of classifier** based on data size and feature dimension. The Bayes/KNN/ANN triad provides the conceptual toolkit for this justification.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bayes Classifier Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Feature Vector x] --> B0[Compute Likelihood P(x / class y)]
    A0 --> C0[Multiply by Prior P class y]
    B0 --> C0
    C0 --> D0[Argmax across all classes y]
    D0 --> E0[Predicted Class y hat]
    F0[Evidence P x as normalizer] -.-> D0
    G0[Training Data Statistics] --> B0
    H0[Class Frequency Counts] --> C0
```

### 4.2 KNN Classification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q0[Query Point xq] --> M0[Choose Distance Metric d]
    M0 --> S0[Compute d xq xi for all training points]
    S0 --> R0[Sort distances ascending]
    R0 --> T0[Select K smallest distances]
    T0 --> V0[Majority Vote of K labels]
    V0 --> P0[Predicted Class y hat]
    D0[Training Dataset stored in memory] --> S0
    K0[Hyperparameter K] --> T0
```

### 4.3 Artificial Neural Network Architecture (MLP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1[Input x1] --> H1A[Hidden Neuron 1A]
    I2[Input x2] --> H1A
    I3[Input x3] --> H1A
    I1 --> H1B[Hidden Neuron 1B]
    I2 --> H1B
    I3 --> H1B
    I1 --> H1C[Hidden Neuron 1C]
    I2 --> H1C
    I3 --> H1C
    H1A --> H2A[Hidden Neuron 2A]
    H1B --> H2A
    H1C --> H2A
    H1A --> H2B[Hidden Neuron 2B]
    H1B --> H2B
    H1C --> H2B
    H2A --> O1[Output Class 1 prob]
    H2B --> O1
    H2A --> O2[Output Class 2 prob]
    H2B --> O2
    H2A --> O3[Output Class 3 prob]
    H2B --> O3
    O1 --> SOFT[Softmax Layer]
    O2 --> SOFT
    O3 --> SOFT
    SOFT --> PRED[Predicted Class y hat]
```

### 4.4 Backpropagation Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FWD[Forward Pass: Compute activations layer by layer] --> LOSS[Compute Loss L cross entropy or MSE]
    LOSS --> BP[Backpropagation: Chain rule to compute gradients]
    BP --> GRADW[Gradients dL/dW for each layer]
    GRADW --> UPDATE[Weight Update W new = W old minus eta times gradient]
    UPDATE --> FWD
    PREDCHK{Convergence: Loss below threshold} --> |Yes| STOP[Stop Training]
    PREDCHK --> |No| FWD
```

### 4.5 Comparative Decision Flow for Classifier Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New Classification Task in Robot Vision] --> Q1{Is data abundant greater than 10K samples?}
    Q1 --> |Yes| Q2{Are features high dimensional greater than 100?}
    Q1 --> |No| Q3{Is interpretability critical?}
    Q2 --> |Yes| ANN[Use ANN Deep MLP or CNN]
    Q2 --> |No| Q4{Is local structure important?}
    Q4 --> |Yes| KNN[Use KNN with tuned K]
    Q4 --> |No| ANN
    Q3 --> |Yes| BAYES[Use Naive Bayes]
    Q3 --> |No| Q5{Need calibrated probabilities?}
    Q5 --> |Yes| BAYES
    Q5 --> |No| KNN
```

> **Diagram Reading Note (KTU Examiner's Tip):** When asked to "explain the working of an ANN", students are expected to **draw** a labeled diagram with input layer, hidden layer(s), output layer, weights, and activation function symbols. The mermaid block above is the structural template — replicate it on paper with proper labels for full marks in a 7-mark sub-question.
